responseSet.title: JAPA Prize Announcements
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

JAPA Prize Announcements

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

iv

responseSet.title: Errata

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Errata

In the book review of Isabel Wilkerson's Caste: The Origins of Our Discontents, by Ebony Dennis and Constance E. Dunlap (Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Vol. 69, No. 2, pp. 407-411, DOI: 10.1177/00030651211006743), there are two errors on p. 411. On lines 6, 9, 14, 16, and 19 the word “miscaste” and, on line 19, “miscaste-ing” should read “miscast” (on line 19 “miscasting”). On lines 13-14 the sentence “And this is what happens to the miscaste white person” should read “However, this is not what happens to the miscast white person.

responseSet.title: Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 5
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 5

Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6638@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

In 2018 we began work on a set of reviews of the newly published Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott, inviting an international group of psychoanalysts to each write on one of the volumes. The great surprise we have had in this project is the pleasure it has brought the reviewers. We are all agreed on the importance of this project and of Winnicott's presence and impact on our field, but the pleasure and emotional meaning of every writer's encounter (including ours) were indeed unexpected.

Here, in his review of Volume 5, Emanuel Berman elaborates an important insight into the period of work published in this volume (1955-1959). Berman speaks of two Winnicotts: the innovator and the traditionalist, each found both in formal papers of this period and in letters, where—as Berman notes—Winnicott's tone is by turns amusing, sharp, and “haunted.” Winnicott moves—at once easily and painfully—between new breakthroughs and a comfortable placement in tradition. Berman notes that however evenly Winnicott balanced himself between innovation and existing models, we are at present most often attuned to what is new in his thinking.

Perhaps in Winnicott's rapid oscillations between his profound loyalty to Freud and his radically innovative propositions, he may have been unconsciously enacting the distinction he was to later make between object relating and object use. That his love for Freud could survive his criticisms and deep disagreements allowed that love to become ever more generative as Winnicott embraced his own theoretical authority.

Email: pb6638@gmail.com

619

responseSet.title: Chekhov was a Doctor
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chekhov was a Doctor

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore Jacobs

18 East 87th Street
New York, NY 10128
Email: theojmd@aol.com

Theodore Jacobs  

One winter when I was in medical school, I came across a notice that Frank O'Connor, the great Irish writer, would be teaching a course in short story writing in Harvard's summer session.

I had long been interested in writing, had written a few short stories, and was eager to take O'Connor's course. The requirement for admission was to submit a short story of one's own. I did this and waited to hear if my story had passed muster. Months went by and I heard nothing at all. This was not a good sign, but when the time came I decided to travel to Cambridge in hopes of talking my way into the class.

When I arrived, I found some thirty people in the same boat. All of us wanted to take the course, everyone had submitted a story, and no one had received a response of any kind. O'Connor had read none of the submissions. He was too preoccupied with his own writing and his devotion to Irish malt whiskey.

Faced with selecting a class of twelve from this group of applicants, and doing it on the spot, as the course was to begin the next day, O'Connor decided to interview everyone who had shown up.

Thirty of us lined up outside his office door, and one by one we entered to be assessed by the great man. When my turn came, O'Connor, perched on a stool, looked me over like a curious leprechaun.

“And what is it that you do, son?” he asked.

“I am a medical student,” I replied.

“Glory to Jesus. What 'tis it about the study of medicine that makes you so unhappy that you've got to write?”

“Gross anatomy,” I replied.

“Well. I don't know if you've got a bit of talent on you at a'tall, a'tall. But I'm going to take you into me class and do you have any idea why, lad?”

“None at all.

responseSet.title: Jill Gentile Responds to Nomi Maya Stolzenberg
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Jill Gentile Responds to Nomi Maya Stolzenberg

Jill Gentile

Jill Gentile

26 West 9th Street, Suite 10A
New York, NY 10011
Email: jillgentile3@gmail.com

Jill Gentile  

I am writing in response to Nomi Maya Stolzenberg's recent book essay, “Law, Gender, and the Liberal Project in American Psychoanalytic Thought” (JAPA 68/3), in which she considers my book, Feminine Law: Freud, Free Speech, and the Voice of Desire (with Michael Macrone; Gentile 2016), along with two other books that, in her words, “yoke law and psychoanalysis to generate new understandings of the modern subject and the modern state” (p. 503).

Though I dispute, or at least quibble with and wish to qualify, Stolzenberg's conclusions with respect to Feminine Law (she argues that it offers a vision of liberal hope contra “the fundamentally illiberal world of Lacan”), that is not what compels this letter. Sadly, her thoughtful and complex essay—which, in the main, is generous toward and appreciative of my book's scope and aims—misrepresents key aspects of its argument and so misleads the reader. And because of the complexity of both her essay and my book, to address these misreadings requires a bit of unpacking. Hence the length of this letter.

Promisingly, Stolzenberg, quoting my preface, tells the reader that my work “finds its home in some space among Freud and Jacques Lacan, D. W. Winnicott, Wilfred Bion, Julia Kristeva, Heinz Kohut, Thomas Ogden, and Jessica Benjamin, among many others” (Gentile 2016, p. xx). Stozenberg suggests that I am subjecting political or democratic speech to a “Lacanian analysis” (p. 508), that my understanding of desire is situated in Lacanian thought (p. 512), and that “there is a fundamental mismatch between [my] Lacanian vocabulary and [my] political conclusions and aspirations for the betterment of society …” (p. 516). Stolzenberg seems to forget that my aim is to situate my text in a space between theories. Though I appreciate Lacan's contributions on language and desire, my understanding of desire emanates instead from the intersubjectivity of Winnicott, Benjamin, and Ogden, a point on which I've elaborated at length in my own synthesis over time (see, e.g., Gentile 2001, 2007, 2008a,b, 2010). This synthesis also owes a debt to Peircean semiotics, in which I discovered a powerful resonance with Lacan's seminal contributions, which then influenced me further.

[[RunningHead]]

Nomi Maya Stolzenberg Responds

Nomi Stolzenberg

Nomi Stolzenberg

699 West Exposition Boulevard
Room 476
Los Angeles, CA 90089
Email: nstolzenberg@law.usc.edu

Nomi Stolzenberg  

Jill Gentile's response to my review of her book (JAPA 68/3) raises two important concerns. One is whether it is possible to oppose a phallocentric, masculinist order—be it the symbolic order of psychoanalysis, the legal order, the social order, or the order of language itself—without reproducing traditional gender binaries, even if one's aim is to deconstruct gender essentialism. The second is whether it is possible to synthesize a Lacanian account of desire with the account of intersubjectivity found in object relations and relational psychoanalysis. Conjoined, these two issues challenge us to think more deeply about what it means to dismantle our phallocentric legal and symbolic orders and replace them with a “feminine law”—the title, coinage, and overarching theme of Gentile's book (2016). As I asked in my review, what makes the alternative that Gentile proposes feminine, and what makes it law?

Although Gentile seems to think otherwise, she and I are in agreement that we need to resist the traditional view of law as located exclusively in the realm of reason, which has traditionally been conceptualized as a male preserve. Like her, I believe the qualities that have historically been excluded from the dominant conception of law, the nonrational sides of human experience, historically associated with the female, are not antithetical to law but, rather, constitutive of another kind of law, one less beholden to the Enlightenment project of autonomous individualism and more attuned, as Gentile rightly says, to the need to surrender to desires that are “not of our individual sovereign choosing.

Psychoanalysis Present Tense

Between the World and Me. By Ta-Nehisi Coates. New York: Spiegel & Grau, 2015, 176 pp., $26.00 hardcover, $18.30 paperback.

Aurelio Ogilvie

Aurelio Ogilvie

1002 East Capitol Street NE
Washington, DC 20003
Email: dr.josephogilvie@gmail.com

Aurelio Ogilvie   Advanced Candidate, Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis.

I was invited to review Between the World and Me just as the world plunged headlong into fresh deep realms of darkness and chaos. The novel coronavirus had turned vibrant cities into ghost towns, and ICU wards into war zones. But that wasn't the only pandemic: police in the United States had killed 164 black men and women in the first eight months of 2020 (Cohen 2020). The video-streamed murders of young African American men took place before our eyes. “Karens,” so many of them, took center stage. The name emerged as a derisive term, calling out the blatant racism of white people who ascribe malicious intent to African Americans engaged in everyday activities of life: birdwatching or taking a daily jog. There was Starbucks Karen, Central Park Karen, San Francisco Karen. In each situation, the malevolent actions—a malignant othering—sought to set boundaries as to where it was or wasn't appropriate for the other's body to be present and to suppress the voice of the other—what they had a right to say to a person in the historical majority.

As I began to read Between the World and Me, I painfully remembered when my own body was not my own and my voice could be so easily silenced. I was eighteen and had just moved to my first apartment in Queens. With a newfound sense of independence and optimism for the future, I left my apartment to make a grocery run to cook my first meal. Within ten minutes, an unmarked car accelerated toward me. It jumped the curb and blocked my path. I was paralyzed. My instincts to run were thwarted—thankfully! Two white men jumped out with guns drawn.

One officer grabbed my jacket collar and, using it to choke me, threw me to the ground and pinned me, the gun in his right hand placed at my temple, his knee on my back. The other held a walkie-talkie in one hand and his gun in the other. I pleaded, implored them to listen to me.

In his most recent book, Irwin Hirsch offers a range of thoughts on a controversy that has long roiled the psychoanalytic community—the tensions between an understanding of change in psychoanalysis as deriving most fundamentally from interpretation and insight and a view that it inheres most of all in the corrective powers of a therapeutic relationship. In Hirsch's version, change derives from “the salubrious impact of new experience with the analyst as new and internalized significant other” (p. 237), and “the goal of analysis is not the discovery of buried truth, but rather the living-out and articulation of old internalized interpersonal patterns and the facilitation of new experience” (p. 138; emphasis added).

Developmental Considerations

On Adolescence: Inside Stories. By Margot Waddell. New York: Routledge, New York, 2018, xviii + 282 pp., $48.95 paperback.

Gretchen Schmutz

Gretchen Schmutz

400 North McClurg Court, Suite 3304
Chicago, IL 60611
Email: gschmutz@alumni.uchicago.edu

Gretchen Schmutz   Faculty, Institute for Clinical Social Work, Chicago; former faculty member, Northwestern University Medical School.

Margot Waddell's On Adolescence: Inside Stories offers a fascinating examination of the internal life of the adolescent. By weaving together psychoanalytic theory, clinical vignettes, and works of literature, Waddell demonstrates how fictional characters and the images of poetry vivify the wildly fluctuating self states of adolescents. Her many citations from young adult fiction uniquely elaborate our understanding of the bizarre, chaotic journey this age group must undergo. Waddell focuses more on development than on psychopathology, though she makes clear, through her discussion of theory and her clinical accounts, that the adolescent can be vulnerable to hiccups and dangerous regressions during this difficult period. She helps us appreciate the powerful anxieties experienced when a young person attempts to engage and cope with external reality more and more independently. Waddell teaches the great value of literature for the adolescent reader by exemplifying how stories can concretize and represent the adolescent's divergent subjectivities: the ordinary versus the alien.

Waddell's command of literature is evident in the range and breadth of her literary citations, from Shakespeare to contemporary works. She highlights how much of young adult fiction consists of stories involving aliens, extreme experiences, or tales of survival, and she looks at how stories can capture and help the adolescent integrate what she calls “parallel universes” of the extreme and the ordinary. She notes there is a kind of “capsizing” of the personality as the adolescent tries to integrate newly arriving selves. These developments can include painful feelings like dread and uncertainty, as well as sexual and destructive urges (pp. 204-205). For example, she references The Little Friend by Donna Tartt and quotes a section of the book about Harriet, one of the protagonists.

Sandra Buechler's Psychoanalytic Approaches to Problems in Living is more than just another book one might read about, purchase, skim, and then shelve. This is a volume to be kept near at hand, one to revisit when needing to clarify, at the deepest level, what the nature of our clinical work is and why we do it. An experienced clinician, as well as a wise and generous person, Buechler continues what has been, in her words, “an extended effort to come to terms with mourning, aging, uncertainty, loneliness, and other fundamental challenges” inherent in human experience (p. vii). The title is a nod to one of her clinical forebears, Harry Stack Sullivan, who characterized patients' emotional difficulties as “problems in living.”

Each chapter concerns a “human predicament” (p. 13), such as aloneness, resilience, and mourning, and in each the author considers important attitudes that inform a clinician's work, presented as dichotomies that “seem to regularly evoke contradictory advice from commentators” (p. 50). She is especially interested in attitudes about adaptation vs. protest, intense vs. modulated emotion, and the value of verbal vs. experiential communication.

If the clinician values getting along over engaging in conflict, for example, she may not acknowledge a frustrated reaction to something the patient says or does; if she is more conflict-tolerant, and has compassion for both self and patient, she may bring up an enactment for mutual exploration. Will she see intense expressions of distress as something to be moderated, or simply witnessed? Will she privilege putting every experience into words?

Buechler poses several other dichotomous attitudes—such as remembering vs. moving on, obeying vs. defying rules, avoiding shame vs. exploring it—but whatever those attitudes are, she argues, the clinician needs to have certain core orienting capacities. As she wrote in Clinical Values: Emotions That Guide Psychoanalytic Treatment (2004)

Irene Landsman   Private practice, Garrett Park, MD; participant, New Directions writing program, Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis.

The fractured structure of Joan Wexler's haunting memoir replicates the author's lifelong challenge to make sense of a life that seemed to defy the construction of a coherent narrative. Relationships were always mutable, as was the definition of “home,” and even as she writes her story she notes that it still “almost seems dangerous to try to make sense of things” (p. 113).

The story begins in medias res, when, in 2008, the author picks up the search for her long-missing father. As if looking over her shoulder at the computer screen, we see the “blurry facsimile of his death certificate” come into view, and perhaps catch our breath as she must have done (p. 2). This public record turns out to provide some precious, if sparse, factual information, but raises new unanswered, and perhaps unanswerable, questions.

The first such question, as Wexler tries to obtain an official copy of the document, is that of her “relationship to the deceased.” That in fact is a central question of the book and of the author's life—the definition of relationship. Wexler's father, dead ten years by the time she saw his death certificate, last lived with her when she was two. She has a few memories of visits during the next three years, before, she writes early in the book, “all contact with him ended. There were no letters, no birthday cards, no photographs, and no child support” (p. 3). This summary is at odds with an episode recounted later, illustrating the ongoing difficulty of forming a single account that can encompass all of the relevant information. In fact, she tracked her father down when she was a senior in college (having discovered that he lived not far from her)

This being human is a guest house. Every morning is a new arrival. A joy, a depression, a meanness, some momentary awareness comes as an unexpected visitor. Welcome and entertain them all.

—Jalaluddin Rumi

The great existential psychiatrist Ludwig Binswanger famously pointed out to Freud that therapeutic failure could “only be understood as the result of something which could be called a deficiency of spirit.” Binswanger was surprised when Freud agreed, asserting, “Yes, spirit is everything” (Binswanger 1963, p. 1).

The life of Angelo D'Agostino S.J., M.D. illustrates both Freud's assertion and the sentiment expressed by the thirteenth-century Persian poet Rumi that “this being human is a guest house.” The soulful life of Fr.

[[RunningHead]]

Roaming in the Writer's Mind

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

246 Eliot Street
Chestnut Hill, MA 10467-1447
Email: drfredbusch@gmail.com

Fred Busch  

Over the years I have discovered that I write for various reasons. I say it this way because when I started my psychoanalytic journey I never planned to be a writer, and I didn't know what impelled me to write. At some point I realized that I had started writing—and continued to write—in order to understand something that was puzzling me. Once I was able to find my own mind through psychoanalysis, I realized that I had many questions about assumed wisdom.

I found there was no better way to approach the many questions I had than writing about them. Only in writing am I able to see how an interesting idea—when put to paper is full of vagueness, gaps in logic, or unsubstantiated speculations. Being honest with myself about the clarity of my ideas is one of the most difficult parts of writing. It is easy, sometimes, to both realize an idea needs further elaboration and say to myself, “Oh, it's good enough.” Through work on editorial boards, I found that often a paper submitted to a journal has a thought-provoking idea as its premise, but the author offers fuzzy explanations or ambiguous clinical examples to support an argument. Given my own experience it is not difficult to understand how this happens. It's always helpful to have a colleague who will be frank in offering criticism, and I've been fortunate in this regard.

I believe that over the past forty years I have discovered elements of psychoanalytic technique—and a Freudian theory that explains them—that are central to how psychoanalysis is curative.1 As my understanding has developed through analytic work with patients and by reading other authors, my thinking has expanded from the ideas expressed in my first papers on technique (1992, 1993).2 I have found that when I present my perspective

1See Busch 2014, 2015, 2016.2One of the pleasures of being analysts is the possibility for continued growth and development if we can allow ourselves to face the anxiety of realizing how much we don't know.

responseSet.title: Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 7
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 7

Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6638@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

The Winnicott whom Glen Gabbard encounters in this volume is at the height of his creative and clinical powers yet also in significant distress and conflict. He has, as Gabbard astutely points out, become one of the few psychoanalytically informed public intellectuals consulting to an entire generation of British baby boomers through his frequent pamphlets, radio broadcasts, and public lectures. Simultaneously, Winnicott struggles to find a place for himself in the analytic community, repeatedly challenging Kleinian orthodoxy over the role of actual experience in the organization of the infant's internal world. Winnicott's despair at finding himself a psychoanalytic outsider was matched only by his discomfort at the prospect of his work being seamlessly assimilated by one of the various schools at the British Psychoanalytical Society. Perhaps it was Winnicott's struggle to sustain the isolation necessary for his constantly evolving creativity—alongside his paradoxical need for recognition—that gave rise to his most revolutionary psychoanalytic ideas.

Gabbard shows us that Winnicott's writing from this period demanded nothing less than a reshaping of the entire analytic project. Analysis no longer hinged on the analyst's well-timed interpretations, but was reconceived as a process in which two unique individuals learned over time to listen to one another, and in so doing learned to listen to themselves. Both the individual and the dyad maintain their integrity, yet both are transformed in analytic praxis. Winnicott's papers, letters, and communications in this volume are a significant and central contribution to the unfolding paradigm shift in psychoanalysis: as Ogden and others have characterized it, a phenomenological, ontological turn occurs here, bringing the subjectivity of the analyst—in parallel with the mother's subjectivity—into the treatment space. Harold Searles and others (Edgar Levenson and Philip Bromberg from the interpersonal world) emerge out of this extraordinary, creative moment in Winnicott's career.

[[RunningHead]]

Maternal Desire: On Children, Love, and the Inner Life. 2nd ed. By Daphne de Marneffe. New York: Scribner, 2019, xiv + 291 pp., $18.00 paperback.

Elizabeth Fritsch

Elizabeth Fritsch

6723 Whittier Avenue, Suite 307
McLean, VA 22101
Email: drelizabethfritsch@gmail.com

Elizabeth Fritsch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society; Director, the SPRING Project, a CFS-sponsored outreach initiative that provides therapy for women and families experiencing perinatal mood distress.

An impetus for this enduring book on maternal life was a young mother's loss for words. In Daphne de Marneffe's personally anchored account of maternal experience, she tells us about her sputtering silence when a respected mentor remarked on her “lack of professional productivity” after de Marneffe became a mother. De Marneffe is a clinical psychologist, a psychoanalytic psychotherapist, and the mother of three children. This encounter was soon followed by a conversation with her obstetrician in which de Marneffe found herself saying she had “turned into a fifties housewife.” She felt disturbed by the impact of her mentor's rebuke. She noticed how “strangely inarticulate” she felt about the unfolding and unexpected experience of deep satisfaction in her mothering. De Marneffe's book, Maternal Desire: On Children, Love, and the Inner Life, first published in 2004 and now issued in a second edition, is her effort to give voice to the experience and development of maternal desire. She has updated this edition with illustrative clinical vignettes and a recognition of increased gender role fluidity and family arrangements. She notes that in this new edition she has taken “special care to differentiate the subjective experience of maternal desire from mothers' choices” (p. 4) about time spent with their children. She seeks to offer “a framework of thinking about women's desire to care for their children in a way that is consistent with feminist ideals and free from sentimentality” (p. 21).

While the starting point for her writing is personal, de Marneffe brings her clinical knowledge and a multitude of scholarly sources to this deep dive into the maternal experience. Her writing will likely be helpful to women embarking on motherhood. She provides guideposts to orient those in the proverbial trenches of parenting babies or young children. Her discourse also speaks directly to women and couples struggling with questions of how to balance professional and parenting aspirations. As a psychoanalyst with a focus on helping pregnant and postpartum women whose entry into parenthood is threatened by mood disturbances, I expect that Maternal Desire will also be of use to clinicians.

Psychoanalysis and Geopolitics

The Handbook of Psychoanalytic Holocaust Studies: International Perspectives. Edited by Ira Brenner. New York: Routledge, 2020, xx + 246 pp., $140.00 hardcover, $68.95 paperback.

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

3941 Duncan Place
Palo Alto, CA 94306-4551
Email: nmoshe@gmail.com

Nathan Szajnberg   Former Freud Professor, The Hebrew University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Israel Psychoanalytic Society.

When Bruno Bettelheim emerged from almost one and a half years in the Buchenwald concentration camp, he found refuge at the University of Chicago. There he wrote about his experiences in the Nazi camp, but his article was rejected by psychoanalytic journals, always for one of two reasons. First, the journal editors did not believe him; they did not believe that Germans could do such things. Second, they claimed that since Bettelheim had been in the camp, his report was subjective, and hence not “scientific.” Only Gordon Allport's Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology accepted the paper, which was finally published in October 1943. Sixteen years later, Bettelheim was able to collect himself and publish this as one of his major works, The Informed Heart: Autonomy in a Mass Age (Bettelheim 1960).

Now, some seventy-five years after the Nazi attempt to systematically wipe out all Jews, books on the Shoah proliferate, among them this most recent profound contribution, whose contributors include such luminaries as Ira Brenner, Salman Akhtar, Zvi Lothane, Anna Ornstein, Dori Laub, Vamik Volkan, Peter Loewenberg, Werner Bohleber, Yolanda Gampel, and Ilany Kogan. Such a list should be enough to elicit our attention. Perhaps the book will be read; but if it is, what difference will that make? There is a rise of anti-Jewish sentiment in Europe and the U.S. (let alone its festering in Middle East countries). Nevertheless, the chapters in this volume are compelling. They include painfully personal details of the writers' experience with the Shoah: Ornstein's recounting of her murdered mother's eyes, the death of all her husband's siblings and father; Loewenberg's narrow escape from Germany with a holdover in Shanghai; Ira Brenner's memory of his father's number tattoo from Auschwitz. Here we have psychoanalysts, who have pursued examined lives, speak from an informed heart. Let us listen.

631

responseSet.title: Reply to Mark Solms
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Mark Solms

John Dall'Aglio

John Dall'Aglio

211 Rockwell Hall
Pittsburgh, PA 15282
Email: dallagliojohn@gmail.com

John Dall'Aglio  

I read Mark Solms's commentary with deep appreciation and thanks. Such dialogue is precisely what I hope will arise out of these papers. As the commentary makes clear, linking neuroscience and psychoanalysis opens new frameworks for discussing different analytic views. This is one way neuropsychoanalysis can advance psychoanalytic discourse.

I emphasize again that these papers aim to link Lacanian concepts and neuroscience—not to disprove other theories in favor of a Lacanian view. I therefore welcome Solms's points, as I believe these are genuine avenues of productive dialogue. I will briefly touch on these points, not to remedy the disagreement, but to render the questions as precise as possible, in the hope of facilitating future work.

Is Seeking Sexual?

Solms's clarification of his use of “instinct” and “drive” affords me a similar opportunity. This is especially important, because analytic schools define terms differently. I am grateful to Solms's clear differentiation between drive and instinct in relation to Panksepp's systems. Solms defines drive as the “measure of the demand made upon the ego for work”1 (p. 769) and instinct (an innate prediction) as the inborn motor response to the drive-demand. In contrast, what I refer to as drive includes the ever shifting motor aim (i.e., predictive) component—not demand alone.

I am following Freud's model (1915), in which drive consists of source, pressure, aim, and object; the particular motor pathway (i.e., the aim) is part of drive's anatomy. Of note, the aim and object fail to remove the demand and are sensitized to repeat this failed attempt (see parts 1 and 2). Following Zupančič (2017) and Johnston (2005), I recognize that there may be any number of particular drives—what matters is this formal

1I recognize that Solms is paraphrasing Freud's famous definition here. Lacan's point arises from the part of Freud's definition immediately prior: drive is “the psychical representative” of bodily stimuli (Freud 1915, p. 122). Drive is a frontier-concept indexed to both representation (i.e., the symbolic) and the body (i.e., the real).

[[RunningHead]]

Love in the Time of Cholera. By Gabriel García Márquez, translated by Edith Grossman. New York: Alfred Knopf, 1988.

Michael Krass

Michael Krass

300 North Washington Street, Suite 302-C
Falls Church, VA 22046
Email: mkrassphd@gmail.com

Michael Krass   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Training Institute of the Contemporary Freudian Society; President, Contemporary Freudian Society; Clinical Assistant Professor, The George Washington University.

But that afternoon he asked himself, with his infinite capacity for illusion, if such pitiless indifference might not be a subterfuge for hiding the torments of love.

—Gabriel García Márquez, Love in the Time of Cholera

I read Love in the Time of Cholera when I was twenty-four. It seemed to me a paean to romantic love, the kind that, at that unripe age, I could still idealize. I was also captivated by the adventures of the main characters. I recently read it again. Coincidentally, I read it several months before the 2019 coronavirus showed up in Wuhan. I jumped into the book with the expectation of being taken along for a rollicking ride where the main characters' undying passion for one another would swirl and swoop in and around me. What I found, thirty years' of experience and a psychoanalysis later, was a much more twisted sort of love story than I had remembered it to be, where a melodramatic notion of love, the kind of adolescent—or even infantile—love that likely appealed to me in my young adulthood was pockmarked with acts of cruelty and mendacity, with exploitation and prejudice aimed at those with less power. Despite the pleasure I took in taking in Márquez's vivid writing, I found it hard to get through. I hung in there, perhaps brought along by the book's poeticism and Márquez's way of telling tales that wend in intertwining serpentine strands. But it left me sad and wondering whether I had participated in something hurtful, something exploitative.

812

responseSet.title: What Use is Freud?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Use is Freud?

Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

117 North First Street, Suite 113
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
Email: mshulmanphd@gmail.com

Michael Shulman  

Sigmund Freud, psychoanalysis, psychoanalytic education, sexuality, character, Freudian theory, candidates

More than a hundred years into our field's development, examining Freud's place in psychoanalytic education is timely. What authority does he hold for psychoanalysts in 2021? Is he still the architect, or overseer, of psychoanalysis? Freud has been a metonym for psychoanalysis, yet the history of Freud's identification with the totality of psychoanalysis has had important unfortunate consequences. Negative aspects of this identification subtly linger, interfering in our collective appreciation of post-Freudian theoretical innovations. Too much of psychoanalysis has been “bought at the company store” of Freud's ideas. Though part of this problem is created by idealizations of Freud, much of it stems from Freud's precocious emphasis on psychoanalytic findings within his tripartite definition of psychoanalysis. As a result, many of his theoretical accounts were taken prematurely as definitive building blocks for a comprehensive psychoanalytic theory, when in fact they were only provisional formulations. Presently, portions of Freud's theories are silently withering on the psychoanalytic vine. Data from the PEP-Web archive reveal the declining use of a set of once important, closely linked conceptions—Freud's psychosexual theory and his characterology—and illustrate the kinds of Freudian ideas that have lost their usefulness. The indispensable and enduring elements in his work are identified.

responseSet.title: Sex and Prediction Error, Part 1: The Metapsychology of Jouissance
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex and Prediction Error, Part 1: The Metapsychology of Jouissance

John Dall'Aglio

John Dall'Aglio

211 Rockwell Hall
Pittsburgh, PA 15282
Email: dallagliojohn@gmail.com

John Dall'Aglio  

neuroscience, jouissance, sex, Lacan, real, symbolic, drive

Jouissance is one of Jacques Lacan's most impenetrable concepts. Yet it is essential to Lacan's view of sex. The term is sometimes translated as “enjoyment,” but this misses key features of the concept, notably its “traumatic,” excessive character. This excess points to a structural negativity within the subject (i.e., the real), an original split that cannot be remedied. In this first of a series of three papers, it is proposed that “surplus prediction error”—as understood within contemporary neuropsychoanalysis—is a neural correlate for jouissance. In part 1, jouissance is explicated within Lacanian metapsychology, primarily in reference to Lacan's real and symbolic registers. Jouissance is an excess enjoyment, outside of the binding, representational capacities of language. The real is the negativity or antagonism within the symbolic, the limit of language and meaning, the point where jouissance emerges. To clarify the relationships among these terms, their positions are traced in some of Freud's major concepts, including drive, infantile sexuality, repetition, and the unconscious. A basic understanding of jouissance is necessary for the rest of this Lacanian neuropsychoanalytic project.

[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Study Of Friendship

Amistad: Una hermandad elegida (Friendship: A Chosen Brotherhood). By Luis Kancyper. Buenos Aires: Editorial Lumen, 2014, 296 pp., $19.00 paperback.

Jani Linares

Jani Linares

Avenida San Jerónimo, #962-16
Col. San Jerónimo
Delegación Magdalena Contreras, CP 10200
CDMX
MÉXICO
Email: jani10pp20@gmail.com

Jani Linares   Training Analyst for Children and Adolescents, Mexican Psychoanalytic Association (APM); Latin American Board Member, International Psychoanalytical Association; Director of Community and Culture, FEPAL.

“A friend arrives on time; those who are not friends arrive when they have time” is the opening line of Amistad: Una hermandad elegida. In this book, Luis Kancyper, a true ambassador of Freudian science, amazes us again with his original thoughts and extraordinary capacity to articulate both theory and practice. On this occasion, the author turns his attention and thoughts toward the concept of friendship, defined as a chosen sibling relationship, not imposed by law or by bonds of consanguinity, commanded only by the mutual admiration between two free individuals. A bond devoid of arrogance.

Kancyper, an avid and enthusiastic reader, frames his psychoanalytic thought within a vast cultural knowledge. He has the gift of moving back and forth from literature to the realm of clinical psychoanalysis, crowning each chapter with extensive and rich clinical material. This way of writing enhances considerably his psychoanalytic perspective on the subject of friendship. The book has two parts. The first, “Friendship in the Analytic Process,” comprises four chapters; the second, “Friendship in Literature and Psychoanalysis,” has two. The book closes with an epilogue: “Friendship and Its Five Functions.” While my comments will focus mainly on the first part, I will comment as well on the second part and the epilogue.

The author reminds us that psychoanalysis originated in the friendship between Freud and Fliess, a friendship marked by exceptional creativity.

209

Louis Rose   Professor of Modern European History, Otterbein University; Executive Director, the Sigmund Freud Archives; Honorary Member, American Psychoanalytic Association.

What does it mean to understand mental and social phenomena from within? What, in other words, does it mean to approach such phenomena with empathy? Susan Lanzoni's Empathy: A History explores the many and varied attempts to answer that question. The attempts, Lanzoni argues, have animated and continue to animate key developments in psychological science, counseling, social work, and psychotherapy, including psychoanalysis.

Lanzoni's book is a detailed lexicographic study, tracking the meanings and usages of empathy across multiple disciplines and several decades. By charting the appearance, definition, and application of the term, it provides at the same time a history of professional and academic practices within psychology in particular and the social sciences in general.

Laura Whitman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Clinical Assistant Professor, Columbia University Medical Center; faculty, Psychoanalytic Association of New York.

I chose to review these three books together, as all explore relationships over time. All are informed by psychoanalytic ideas, yet are written for a general audience. My perspective is that of a psychoanalyst who treats children, adolescents, adults, and couples, but also that of a general reader who consumes novels, poetry, biographies, art history, self-help books, blogs, memes, and maps.

The Rough Patch

I'll first discuss The Rough Patch: Marriage and the Art of Living Together, by Daphne de Marneffe. The Rough Patch is meant for those in unhappy marriages, but it will also be helpful to clinicians. De Marneffe is a gifted psychologist and couples therapist whose work is steeped in psychoanalytic thought. She has been especially influenced by the literature on attachment and mentalization. In 2019 she published the second edition of Maternal Desire: On Children, Love, and the Inner Life (2004), in which she explores controversies in the psychology of motherhood.

Winnicott in Topeka: Ego Psychology, American Culture, and Object Relations

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

21 Vicksburg Street
San Francisco, CA 94114
Email: stephen.seligman331@gmail.com

Stephen Seligman  

Winnicott, Menninger, Anna Freud, ego psychology, concern, Wallerstein, religion, development, death instinct, aggression, psychosis, borderline

D. W. Winnicott (1963c) presented “The Development of the Capacity for Concern” at the Topeka Psychoanalytic Society on October 12, 1962.1 The Topeka Society was closely tied to the Menninger Clinics. “Menninger” was the leading psychiatric and psychoanalytic center in the U.S., at a time when analysis was the dominant force on the American mental health scene and prominent in popular culture. Its leaders, Karl and Will Menninger, were the best-known psychiatrists in the nation. The multidisciplinary program included extensive inpatient and outpatient treatment facilities for both adults and children, and large training and research programs. It was also one of the most active centers for the development of ego psychology, which had become the hegemonic orientation in North American analysis. In retrospect, the discussions of Winnicott's paper at the Topeka Society, by Robert Wallerstein (1962) and H. G. van der Waals (1962), reflect the ambition, promise, and limitations of that paradigm and its complex relations with the British object relations schools.

1For a close reading of this paper, see my other contribution to this issue, pp. 491-512.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, University of California, San Francisco; Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Clinical Professor, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Two Takes on Wilkerson's Caste

Caste: The Origins of Our Discontents. By Isabel Wilkerson. New York: Random House, 2020, xviii + 476 pp., $32.00 hardcover.

Ebony Dennis and Constance Dunlap

Constance Dunlap

1801 Primrose Road NW
Washngton, DC 20012
Email: ebonydennis@yahoo.com

2015 R Street NW, Suite 302
Washington, DC 20009
Email: drconstancedunlap@gmail.com

Constance Dunlap   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, The George Washington University; Distinguished Fellow, American Psychiatric Association; Advisory Board member, Clinical Psychiatry News.

Caste: The Origins of Our Discontents is the second book by Pulitzer Prize-winner Isabel Wilkerson, best-selling author of The Warmth of Other Suns: The Epic Story of America's Great Migration (2010). As with her first book, Caste is an historically illuminating literary work that contributes to our understanding of how Black Americans have come to regard ourselves in society. In Caste, Wilkerson organizes her thoughts into seven parts, proceeding from “The Eight Pillars of Caste” to “Awakening.” Here we will address four of those parts. Wilkerson follows in the great tradition of African American writers and her fellow alumni of Howard University, including Paul Lawrence Dunbar, Zora Neale Hurston, Toni Morrison, Amiri Baraka, and Ta-Nehisi Coates, who write of the American experience through lenses unclouded by the dominant culture's impingements and delusions.

Addressing the realities of racism is a heavy lift. Wilkerson accomplishes it by enlisting relatable metaphors throughout the book to speak in displacement. With this approach, she creates space in which one can think and possibly gain deeper knowledge of the emotionally charged topics of racism and casteism. This potential space is important because exploration of these topics understandably raises defenses that can hinder thinking. Within this space, Wilkerson links the caste systems of India, the U.S., and Nazi Germany. She introduces material, largely unfamiliar to the general reader, about Hitler's envy and admiration of America's ability to legally establish a system of racial oppression. Wilkerson notes that Hitler “attributed” this feat to America's “Aryan stock.” Nazi Germans were further inspired by America's near-genocide of indigenous peoples, including the “exiling” of the survivors to reservations, as well as by the institution of slavery.

responseSet.title: Words' Unconditional Presence
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Words' Unconditional Presence

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

Donald Moss  

When I was four years old, I was immobilized and isolated in a hospital room at the end of a corridor. Being at the end of the corridor meant to me that I was as sick as a living person could be. I had reached the end of the line. Get worse and I would be dead.

I had no one to talk to so I spent a lot of time doing arithmetic problems in my head. I'd try to make the problems difficult and, no matter their difficulty, I could solve each one. This brought me enormous relief. I thought that as long as I could solve these problems, I was okay and would stay alive.

My relationship to numbers didn't exactly save my life but it reassured me that the one I had would continue. I could hold on to numbers and they would work a certain way, no matter what. Know how they work and they were there for me, permanently. As long as they were there, I would be there.

I've never stopped feeling that sense of precarity, the permanent threat of being placed there at the end of the line. And along with that, I've never stopped wanting to attach myself to things that will work in certain predictable ways, no matter what. Some time back, words—there and permanent—took the place once occupied by numbers. Words never vanish; they never trick me. I piggyback on their permanence. We're in this together—words and me—and, in affirmation of that, I write. When I do write, I feel protective toward words, feel they should be treated properly, kindly. I want to establish a relation of mutual care.

I can focus on a concrete situation. Along with many of us, at this moment I am really bothered by whiteness, what it is, what it makes me, what it makes me make of others, etc.—all of it a cultural/personal/theoretical/political mess—too much to either handle or dismiss.

Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; co-founder of the Green Gang, a four-member collective working with climate change denial and the relation between the human and the natural worlds; Chair, Program Committee, American Psychoanalytic Association.

The Historian's Self-Reflection and American Racism

Peter Loewenberg

Peter Loewenberg

449 Levering Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90024-1909
Email: peterl@ucla.edu

Peter Loewenberg  

confidentiality of editorial review process, racism, lynching, slavery, American Holocaust

In March 1997 a distinguished historian, Professor Joel Williamson of the University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill, wrote a self-reflective essay in the Journal of American History (JAH) in which he examined why he, as a born and bred Southerner, could not see and in fact denied and obviated the existence of lynching in American history. The JAH, with an exemplary psychodynamic introduction by editor David Thelen, persuaded six referees, all distinguished scholars, four white and two African American, to waive their strict confidentiality and publish their reviews. The author published the paper as submitted, with no revisions. This unique view of the workings of the academic publication review process reveals a sharp clash in evaluation between the referees based on race and stance toward the self-reflective intent of the author.

[[RunningHead]]

The Evolution of Racism in the Western World: Addressing Fear of the Other

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 65th Street
New York, NY 10065
Email: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

Leon Hoffman   CO-Director, Pacella Research Center, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Chief Psychiatrist, West End Day School, New York.

In 1968 the Kerner Commission on civil disorders warned that racism was causing America to move “toward two societies, one black, one white—separate and unequal … with ‘white racism’ chiefly to blame for the explosive conditions that sparked riots in American cities.” It also warned that a policy of separatism then advocated by many black militants “can only relegate Negroes to a permanently inferior economic state.”

In 1993 Kenneth Clark, an African American psychologist who, along with his wife, presented psychological evidence in the 1954 Supreme Court case Brown v. Board of Education, reminisced at the age of seventy-six: “Reluctantly, I am forced to face the likely possibility that the U.S. will never rid itself of racism and reach true integration. … I shudder at how naïve we all were in our belief in the steady progress racial minorities would make through programs of litigation and education. … I am forced to recognize that my life, in fact, has been a series of glorious defeats” (quoted in Mills 1998, p. 172). We are, in fact, two societies. Liberal New York City, for example, has one of the most segregated school systems in the country, both racially and economically.1 Covid-19 is affecting communities of color much more severely than white communities, Black men are being killed by police, and we are witnessing the use of racial “dog whistles.”

Do whites fear that their place at the head of the table is threatened by nonwhites, who will become the majority in the U.S. as early as 2045? The ruthless treatment of nonwhites by the dominant white Europeans is historically undisputed. Some say that this continues today, despite legal prohibitions, with prisons as the contemporary version of Black slavery.

1School District 15 in Brooklyn has made plans to fully integrate its middle schools after many years of working on it (https://www.nytimes.com/2020/08/20/podcasts/nice-white-parents-school.html). Can this be a model for other school districts?389

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Race, and Racism: Introduction to Japa 69/2

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsommd@berkeley.edu

Mitchell Wilson  

“Maman, look, a Negro; I'm scared!” Scared! Scared! Now they were beginning to be scared of me. I wanted to kill myself laughing, but laughter had become out of the question.

—Franz Fanon, Black Skin, White Masks

Fanon is on a train, and there is the matter of where to sit. In a voice that is unmistakable—-aghast is the word that comes to mind—-he tells us, “I couldn't take it any longer, for I already knew there were legends, stories, history. … As a result, the body schema, attacked in several places, collapsed, giving way to an epidermal racial schema” (1952, p. 92).

Fanon's work, published mainly in two books over the course of his brief adult life, confronts the reader with the fact that he lives what he describes and theorizes, and that autobiography and sociography are coextensive; they are inextricably linked. The “psycho-affective” realm that Fanon writes about is neither subjective nor objective, but is a place of social and psychic mediation. This point, as we know, is not entirely foreign to psychoanalysts. Erich Fromm, Herbert Marcuse, Nancy Chodorow, and more recently Lynne Layton and Nancy Hollander (among others) have each ventured into the nexus of the effects of social structure, ideology, and power relations on the individual psyche. We might call these efforts, following Layton, “social psychoanalysis” (2020).

In truth, mainstream analysts, working in their offices and other clinical settings, have never comfortably embraced this work of social psychoanalysis. One finds a history of marginalization of both the ideas and the scholars who wrote in this vein (Erik Erikson comes immediately to mind). And yet the deep penetration of ideology, racism, and power relations into seemingly the most neutral notions of analytic theory and practice is a plain fact. Consider the psychosexual stages of development and the patently heteronormative biases that underwrite them. Or the appellative “homosexual.” Or the term “borderline.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 6

Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6638.gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

Peter Goldberg's deeply felt essay on Volume 6 of Winnicott's Collected Works (1960-1963) presents us an expansively conceived discussion of core Winnicottian developmental and clinical principles. In his rich, evocative review, Goldberg explores Winnicott's concepts of early holding, and of the vicissitudes of affect—necessarily including security in concert with aggression—that emerge in human development. Countering the shallow criticisms of Winnicott as insufficiently attuned to aggression, Goldberg vigorously illustrates how Winnicott's engagement with human destructiveness is central to his clinical and developmental theories. His essay ties Winnicott's clinical theory to contemporary phenomenological accounts of countertransference as a creative and transformative experience—a moment when the analyst may fall ill with many of the same forces and torments that have befallen the patient. Goldberg notes that Winnicott's aim was to help the patient find a way to live, not to cure an illness.

Goldberg focuses not only the theory but also on the malaise of Winnicott's postwar audiences, and he asks us to consider Winnicott as a psychoanalyst-activist. The enrichment of our understanding of Winnicott—the person, the ambitious scholar, the activist, and the clinician—is a welcome event, celebrated here and in reviews of other volumes of the Collected Works.

Email: pb6638.gmail.com

Jeremy Elkins

Jeremy Elkins   Associate Professor of Political Science, Bryn Mawr College; advanced clinical candidate, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia.

This is a rich and diverse collection of papers on trauma and trauma theory, written from a variety of perspectives, including psychoanalysis, philosophy, cultural studies, and religious studies. For the book's editors, it is also an attempt to grapple with some central challenges of trauma theory. To appreciate the nature of these challenges, it is necessary to understand what is meant by “trauma theory,” which is not the same thing as the theory of trauma.

The theory of psychic trauma has a long and continuous history.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Can Proteanism Be Grounded?

Robert Lifton

Robert Lifton

1051 Riverside Drive
New York, NY 10032
Email: rjl2151@cumc.columbia.edu

Robert Lifton  

Over the course of my work I have described the protean self and collective proteanism as expressions of the human capacity for fluidity and many-sided behavior (Lifton 1993). The term derives from the Greek sea god Proteus, a talented and wily shapeshifter. In a recent article in this journal (Lifton 2020) I invoked the idea of proteanism as a form of openness and flexibility, in contrast to the fixity of cultism, and of cultist Trumpism. As I wrote then, “the protean self is many-sided rather than monolithic, resilient rather than unbending. … [and] is consistent with psychological work over recent decades that stresses the self's fluidity rather than fixity (Gergen 1991)” (p. 427).

Yet I have come to recognize that proteanism can have its own conflicts, having to do with the limits and complexities of shapeshifting.

An awkward but pithy personal encounter I had in the 1970s did much to raise the issue for me. At a large academic reception in New York I was accosted by a graduate student who said something like “Hey Lifton, I tried being a protean man and it doesn't work!” While his brashness got my attention, he seemed more disappointed than hostile. I was immediately struck by the problem he raised. My mumbled reply was along the lines of proteanism being a tendency rather than an absolute, which was true enough but hardly an adequate response.

Mumford and Tillich

As I thought about the issue the student raised, I remembered an essay I had read by Lewis Mumford (1964), the humanist and social critic, on the subject of Freud and Jung.

Lecturer in Psychiatry, Columbia University; Distinguished Professor Emeritus, John Jay College and the Graduate Center, City University of New York.

Submitted for publication May 28, 2021.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Medical Student Education: Introducing an Elective Course

William Badenhorst and Alla Rubitel

Alla Rubitel

19-21 Crawford Street
London W1H 1PJ
UNITED KINGDOM
Email: william@badenhorst.london

8 Fitzjohn's Avenue
London NW3 5NA
UNITED KINGDOM
Email: arubitel@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Alla Rubitel  

Our purpose was to design and implement an elective course in psychodynamic psychiatry, drawing on existing resources and using a psychoanalytic perspective to help synthetize science, the humanities, and clinical practice.

Background

Psychiatry has a long tradition of drawing on the humanities to understand mental functioning and individual experience (Engel 1980; Shlozman 2017). In addition, the importance of developing personal and professional qualities such as empathy has long been recognized in medical education (Quince et al. 2016). Specifically, the arts have been used to promote self-reflection and empathy (Graham et al. 2016; Potash et al. 2014).

Specialty Choice Placements were developed in response to the U.K. General Medical Council's Tomorrow's Doctors (1993), which encouraged medical schools to broaden their range of clinical experience through optional (elective) placements. In 2018, Imperial College London expanded its placements in the penultimate year of its medical degree program, creating the opportunity to offer a new course in psychodynamic psychiatry. This innovation combined clinical learning in psychiatry with an immersion in the arts, using a psychoanalytic approach as an organizing framework.

William Badenhorst, Honorary Senior Clinical Lecturer, Imperial College London. Alla Rubitel, Honorary Clinical Senior Lecturer, Imperial College London.

Submitted for publication March 9, 2021.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mother-Infant Observations: A View into the Wordless Social Instincts That Form the Foundation of Human Psychodynamics

Daniel Benveniste

Daniel Benveniste

4127 205th Avenue SE
Sammamish, WA 98075
Email: daniel.benveniste@gmail.com

Daniel Benveniste  

mother-infant observation, ethology, social instincts

Mother-infant observations attune the psychotherapist to the nonverbal interactions that shape the child's experience of the world. The origins of our interest in psychoanalytic mother-infant observations can be traced back to clinical work with adults, child analyses, ethology (the study of animal behavior), and theoretical questions about the development of the symbolic function in infancy. More recently, seminars and direct experience in mother-infant observation have been gaining popularity as components of psychoanalytic training. Indeed, mother-infant observations are a kind of human ethological investigation that offer a rare peek into the wordless social instincts that find their origins in the ancient evolution of our species.

Rachel Widawsky  

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Series of Reviews

This issue of the Review of Books begins with the fourth installment in a series on The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott, curated by guest editors Philip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris. Volume 4—splendidly reviewed here by Ofra Eshel—covers several intensely productive years in Winnicott's writing life (1952-1955).

Relational Perspectives

In his review of Steven Stern's Needed Relationships and Psychoanalytic Healing: A Holistic Relational Perspective on the Therapeutic Process, Peter Rudnytsky guides us through Stern's reflections on the contemporary psychoanalytic relationship. What does the contemporary psychoanalyst, he asks, have to offer patients who seek “a new relational experience”? Stern's book, Rudnytsky tells us, is “at once a down-to-earth guide to the perplexed analyst trying to ‘create a life together’ with his patient, the purpose of which ‘is to help the patient work out a better life,’ or even ‘have a life at all,’” and “a soaring commentary on our prevailing confusion of tongues after the collapse of the Freudian tower.” Rudnytsky spotlights the unique importance of analytic “fittedness” and reflects on how the work of analysis heals: “the irreducible mystery of the analytic encounter must be apprehended not only with the instruments of reason but also by our emotional participation, since only the two together can bring about the mutual transformation that is the very essence of healing.”

In her review of Robert Grossmark's The Unobtrusive Relational Analyst, Debra Neumann cites Tranströmer's “The Clearing” (1987), quoted by Grossmark: “Deep in the forest there is an unexpected clearing which can be reached only by someone who has lost his way.

From the Editors

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

Reflections on Race and Caste

In a post to the American Psychoanalytic Association Members list-serve (12/26/2019), Dorothy Holmes wrote, “For me, the most attractive aspect of our discipline is that we are a radical, progressive profession that seeks liberation from bondage.” With this powerful invocation, Holmes invites us to consider the many ways that psychoanalysis has been exclusionary and has discouraged participation by many groups. Psychoanalysts have held misguided and at times harmful views of racial diversity, sexual orientation, and gender identity. We dedicate this issue of JAPA Review of Books to inquiry into the nature of racism and caste, white fragility, and fears of the “not-me.”

We are pleased to introduce this theme with Leon Hoffman's “The Evolution of Racism in the Western World: Addressing Fear of the Other.” Hoffman sets out to explore the developmental, historical, and current-day roles of racism in this country. He asks, “Do whites fear that their place at the head of the table is threatened by nonwhites, who will become the majority in the U.S. as early as 2045?” Hoffman recognizes that psychoanalysis—“developed within a liberal culture that assumed white supremacy, with the standard being the white individual”—fails to adequately understand and integrate into analytic practice Black lived experience; to discuss racism among ourselves and with our patients; and to recognize and confront our own racism. Hoffman's essay also examines the origins, pervasiveness, and relentless persistence of racism in our culture at large. He draws on several books, including White Fragility: Why It's So Hard for White People to Talk about Racism (2018), by the education professor and diversity consultant Robin DiAngelo; three volumes by the philosopher Charles Mills, including Black Rights / White Wrongs: The Critique of Racial Liberalism (2017); Martha Nussbaum's The Monarchy of Fear: A Philosopher Looks at Our Political Crisis (2019)

[[RunningHead]]

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Series of Reviews (Continued)

This issue of JAPA Review of Books begins with the fourth installment in a series of reviews of The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott, curated by guest editors Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris. In his review of Volume 5, Emanual Berman presents “a tale of two Winnicotts”—the “daring and original revolutionary” Winnicott, and the loyalist to classical psychoanalysis. We expect that you'll find Berman's writing as engaging as the works it covers, from the years 1955-1959.

Psychoanalysis and Geopolitics

“Never forget” is an oft-repeated invocation of the Holocaust, spoken in part as precaution, in part as prayer. In our present context—pandemic-driven isolation and loss, a long-due reckoning with racial and caste injustices, and a rise of violence against minority groups—a deep look at the Holocaust, through the eyes of those who remember it or whose memories have been shaped in its shadow, has vivid and poignant resonance. In a review of Ira Brenner's Handbook of Psychoanalytic Holocaust Studies, Nathan Szajnberg writes from the perspective of one whose very existence beats the odds. As the child of a Holocaust survivor, Szajnberg invites the reader to hear the voices of the psychoanalyst-authors of this edited volume, “who have pursued examined lives” and “speak from an informed heart.” Highlighting several of the chapters in Brenner's Handbook, Szajnberg focuses particularly on a section addressing the question of how to understand the Nazi mind, and the extreme conditions under which moral rules are shattered. Of the contributors to this volume, Szajnberg writes, “we should thank them for their candor, thoughtfulness, and their wish to help us understand the unspeakable.”

Richard Waugaman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Georgetown University; Training and Supervising Analyst Emeritus, Washington Baltimore Psychoanalytic Institute.

Psychoanalysts should be proud that a colleague has been nominated five times for the Nobel Peace Prize. That distinguished psychoanalyst, winner of the 2015 Sigourney Prize and author or editor of more than fifty books, is Vamik Volkan, who has broadened the psychoanalytic theory of large group dynamics to achieve original insights into the unconscious origins of chronic ethnic conflicts. He has risked his life in an often successful effort to help resolve those conflicts.

Psychoanalysis in Latin America

Artesanías Psicoanalíticas (Psychoanalytic Craftwork). Edited by Willy Baranger, Raquel Zak de Goldstein, & Néstor Goldstein. Buenos Aires: Ediciones Kargieman, 2014, 462 pp., $41.00 paperback.

Rodrigo Barahona

Rodrigo Barahona

1685 Beacon Street, Suite 3
Brookline, MA 02445
Email: rodbarahona@gmail.com

Rodrigo Barahona   Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Board Member, Boston Group for Psychoanalytic Studies.

In reading Raul Hartke's “The Oedipus Complex: A Confrontation at the Central Cross-Roads of Psychoanalysis” (2016), I came across a reference to a piece by Willy Baranger on the oedipus complex from the late seventies that I had never heard of and that immediately stirred my curiosity. Intrigued, and encouraged by a similarly intrigued colleague, I hunted it down and discovered that it has never been translated into English and, to my knowledge, is available only where originally published in 1976, in Revista de psicoanálisis, a publication of the Argentinian Psychoanalytic Association. For reasons of which I am unaware, issues of this journal disappear from the PEP database beginning in 1970, and the only other place a reader might find a copy of this brilliant treatise on Klein's and Freud's Oedipus is in Baranger's Artesanías Psicoanalíticas, edited in 1995 and republished in 2014 with colleagues Raquel Zak de Goldstein and Néstor Goldstein. Here the reader will encounter a trove of invaluable writing gathered together by a small group of Latin America's most erudite and penetrating psychoanalytic minds.

Granted, this book is mostly Willy Baranger's1; he is the sole author of fifteen of its twenty-four chapters. The remaining nine chapters were written with the Goldsteins, Nora R. de Bisi, Hector Garbadino, Jorge Galeano Muñoz, and Gela Rosenthal.

1Willy Baranger published four books in his lifetime, the present one in 1994, the year he died. The other three are Problemas del campo psicoanalitico (Problems of the Psychoanalytic Field), with Madeleine Baranger in 1969; Posicíon y objeto en la obra de Melanie Klein (Position and Object in the work of Melanie Klein) in 1971; and Aportaciones al concepto de objeto en psicoanálisis (Notes on the Concept of Object in Psychoanalysis) in 1980, republished in 2001.647

[[RunningHead]]

The Minds of Patient and Analyst

The Growth of Mind. By Neville Symington. New York: Routledge, 2019, iv + 118 pp., $112.00 hardcover, $34.95 paperback.

Steven Demby

Steven Demby

465 Henry Street
Brooklyn, NY 11231
Email: dembyphd@outlook.com

Steven Demby   Member, Contemporary Freudian Society.

In this slender volume, Neville Symington, who died in 2019 at age eighty-two, surveys the main psychoanalytic viewpoints he has espoused over the course of his career. In an earlier book, A Different Path: An Emotional Autobiography (2018), Symington writes, “It has always been extremely important to me to be taught by someone who knows the subject ‘from the inside,’ who has personally assimilated it, for whom the subject has come as an answer to questions which are of personal significance” (p. 120). One senses that the psychoanalytic themes he explores in The Growth of Mind are ones he knows “from the inside” and have developed out of his personal life struggles, some of which he describes briefly.

Symington was an ordained Catholic priest before rejecting the priesthood and becoming a psychoanalyst. In this book, he recounts how at age twenty-one, during his first year at seminary, he experienced a profound sense of relief, as if he had “arrived home,” when he attended a lecture on ontology by a professor who was “talking from his own soul”: “I was as gripped by his every word just as much as the addict is magnetized to his drug. At the end of that year a foundation had been laid in my heart and mind which has never left me” (p. 85).

Despite rejecting the priesthood, Symington preserves what he found meaningful in his religious education and carries this over into his psychoanalytic approach—a sense of awe at the wonder of Being and the mystery at the heart of existence. In this book, Symington quotes liberally from theologians, as well as from philosophers ranging from Spinoza to Wittgenstein. He is an intuitive thinker, and the strengths and weaknesses of his approach follow from this. The psychoanalytic themes he presents are not developed systematically but reappear as leitmotifs throughout the text.

655

The Analyst's Reveries: Explorations in Bion's Enigmatic Concept. By Fred Busch. New York: Routledge, 2019, vi + 106 pp., $160.00 hardcover, $49.95 paperback.

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomindale Road
White Plains, NY 10605
Email: okernber@med.cornell.edu

Otto Kernberg   Kernberg, Director, Personality Disorders Institute, New York Presbyterian Hospital, Westchester Division, White Plains; Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

The concept of the analyst's reveries has been widely used and emphasized by psychoanalytic scholars, not only within the neo-Bionian school, but also by analysts following other theoretical approaches, including many relationalists. Views and uses of the concept differ widely as various authors apply them to clinical experience. Fred Busch has decided to clarify the concept of reverie and how it is used by different authors, and to explore, in the process, what in it represents a new way of looking at the analyst's work. That task, indeed, seems an important undertaking, and Fred Busch, in this slim volume, carries it out brilliantly and masterfully.

As Busch explores the meanings of the term reverie in Bion's writings, he encounters what direct quotations from Bion reveal as a rather vague generalization of the mother's function in responding to the infant's part in their interaction, transforming the infant's projection of sense impressions into emotions. Bion uses the term beta elements for the raw sense impressions that, with the mother's emotional approach to the infant—be it love or hatred—are transformed into emotions or alpha elements that the infant can internalize as understanding of his own emotions, originally represented by his projected raw sense data or beta elements. The mother's function in transforming beta elements into full-fledged emotions constitutes what Bion calls the “alpha function,” a product of the mother's transformational capacity.

660

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

In this issue, we are pleased to present several essays that we hope will speak to the challenges of post-pandemic reckoning—with ourselves and with each other, and in conversations emerging within our profession—as we each seek to make sense of what the past year has wrought. As psychoanalysts, we have a unique obligation to bring our attention, curiosity, and analytic thought to the troubles that have laid themselves bare through the wreckage of Covid-19. In the spirit of such inquiry, we are pleased to introduce an occasional series of reviews and essays called Psychoanalysis Present Tense. Here we inaugurate the series with three reviews whose authors each train a psychoanalytic eye on books addressing topics of pressing contemporary importance. We invite readers to contact us with suggestions for this series, on topics including race, class, and caste; climate change; economic inequality; shifting conceptions of gender and sexuality; and the impact of technology on human experience.

We are also excited to announce an occasional series called Classics Re-Viewed, in which we will spotlight classic works and explore their contemporary influence. Please watch future issues for the first entry in this series: an essay in which Michael Parsons revisits the work of Enid Balint—a major presence in his early career—from the perspective of his own psychoanalytic maturity. Readers are encouraged to bring us their suggestions for this series as well.

We hope that these two series—one forward-looking, the other historically focused—will bring the intellectual foundations of psychoanalysis into conversation with the urgent demands of the present moment. We look forward to hearing from you.

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Series of Reviews (Continued)

This issue of Review of Books begins with the sixth installment in a series on The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott, curated by guest editors Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris.

Theory and Technique

Beginning Analysis: On the Processes of Initiating Psychoanalysis. By Bernard Reith, Mette Møller, John Boots, Penelope Crick, Alain Gibeault, Ronny Jaffè, Sven Lagerlöf, and Rudi Vermote. New York: Routledge, 2018, xvi + 208 pp., $145.00 hardcover, $41.95 paperback.

Jérémie Richard

Jérémie Richard

3724 McTavish Street
Montreal, QC H3A 1Y2
CANADA
Email: jeremie.richard@mail.mcgill.ca

Jérémie Richard   Doctoral candidate in Counselling Psychology, McGill University Department of Educational and Counselling Psychology; graduate researcher, International Centre For Youth Gambling Problems and High-Risk Behaviors, McGill University; guest member, Canadian Psychoanalytic Society, Quebec English Branch.

Beginning Analysis presents a robust and original attempt at using qualitative research methods to investigate the processes and core dynamics underlying initial interviews in psychoanalysis. This book begins by providing a detailed foundation of a research project conducted as part of the European Psychoanalytical Federation's Ten-Year European Scientific Initiative, in which the Working Party on Initiating Psychoanalysis (WPIP) was created to investigate “the specific dynamics of preliminary interviews, how psychoanalysts work with them, and what it is in these dynamics that leads a potential patient to enter full analysis (or not)” (p. 4). Written by an international team of eight senior psychoanalysts and members of the WPIP, the book compiles the work of hundreds of psychoanalysts who contributed their time and attention to investigating case material from initial psychoanalytic interviews. Analysis of the results presented here not only challenged the authors' initial hypotheses, but also led to the creation of novel theoretical concepts that encourage further exploration, research, and reflection. As stated by the authors, it is crucial to understand how psychoanalysts really work, not simply how we would like to think they work (p. 3). I believe the authors have achieved this goal through their detailed, methodologically sound, and thoroughly elaborated work. Reading this book has encouraged me, as a clinician-in-training, to consider the unconscious dynamics underlying initial interviews and overcome my apprehension regarding initial sessions.

1007
