From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

A mid the swirling issues of the day, we can too easily lose track of what—in the lengthening history of psychoanalysis—remains vital over time. As our ongoing series of reviews of Winnicott's Collected Works shows,1 analysts writing decades ago can seem prescient; we may, in our zeal to build toward the future, overlook the groundwork they have laid. Like a well-loved novel, a psychoanalytic text yields new meanings as a reader matures.

Classics Re-Viewed

In this issue of JAPA Review of Books, we present “Classics Re-Viewed”—a new, occasional feature in which we explore the enduring influence of classic works. We are delighted to inaugurate this series with a stellar exemplar of the form: Michael Parsons's essay on Enid Balint's Before I Was I: Psychoanalysis and the Imagination. For Parsons, the project is richly resonant; “Enid,” he writes, “was an important figure for me personally in my psychoanalytic life.” Balint supervised Parsons's first training case and, together, he and Juliet Mitchell helped Balint edit the collection of papers the book comprises. Here, from the perspective of twenty-eight years after its publication, Parsons takes stock of Balint's influence—some of it long unconscious, and seen only in retrospect—on his development as a psychoanalyst. He also appraises Balint's contributions with a contemporary eye, finding much that is of enduring value (and an occasional mar, perhaps not visible at the time with the tools then available to Balint).

Luis Ripoll   Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai Medical Center; board member, Psychoanalytic Research Consortium.

In her latest work, Jessica Benjamin offers a creative account of how the mind's interaction with other minds structures meaning within the interpersonal field and across development. Grounded in relational psychoanalytic theory, critical theory, and mother-infant observational research, Benjamin's writing unites disparate ideas into a cogent theoretical frame, described succinctly as recognition theory. Initially, this reminded me of my earliest exposure to psychoanalysis, before I had entered psychoanalytic training or engaged in any clinical work, in studies in the history of moral philosophy. This course of study grounded psychoanalytic conceptualizations of morality in Alexandre Kojève's influential reading of Hegel (Kojève 1947), wherein right and wrong are neither rational, divinely inspired categories that exist a priori, to be understood only through pure reason, nor qualities discernible only a posteriori, through an objective appraisal of perceptual data. Rather, right and wrong are themselves categories susceptible to the changing and seemingly irrational dynamics of master and slave, ways of relating and understanding one another that necessarily shift across human history in response to changing contexts. I came to understand psychoanalytic theory as a way of tracing a similar historical contingency at the level of an individual's history: a person develops convictions about right and wrong based on the idiosyncratic, embodied experience of inhabiting characteristic familial roles of motherhood, fatherhood, and/or childhood, each with varying aspects of mastery or servitude. Psychoanalytic theory offered a means of understanding how any of these individuals negotiate such roles, in order to get what they want while also feeling acknowledged by significant others in their subjectivity and agency.

[[RunningHead]]

A Magic Carpet Ride

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

1824 Alderbrook Road NE
Atlanta, GA 30345
Email: slevy02@emory.edu

Steven Levy  

Silly grandpa. Words are nothing. They are just sounds people make when they want to tell somebody something.” On reflecting about my writing activity during my professional career, I reach immediately for the obvious. Because I am a full-time academic, the publish or perish mantra was instilled in me early and forcibly by my mentors, most of whom were themselves prolific writers with whom I identified. Publication was about advancement and establishing my bona fides with my fellow faculty members and students. It was also about making a mark in professional circles. I published two papers in JAPA as a psychoanalytic candidate, at least in part to legitimize my attendance at APsaA meetings. I remember fondly connecting with my regular gang of young author friends at the Waldorf in New York. We rarely mingled with the elderly, but our scholarly writing helped create a feeling of togetherness and belonging. The idea of writing as a way of connecting has remained a pivotal motive throughout my career.

I view myself primarily as an educator, and most if not all of my writing has been driven at least to some degree by my teaching activities. Writing about subjects that I teach has helped me feel articulate in the classroom, in discussions with colleagues, and as a supervisor and mentor.

In looking back over my written output as a psychoanalyst and academic, what immediately stands out to me is that almost every manuscript, whether paper or book, focuses on a single word or concept that I explore and attempt to clarify. The impetus for many of my publications has been an instance of hearing someone use those words or concepts in a way that I thought was imprecise, misleading, contradictory, overreaching, wrong. “Emptiness,” “empathy,” “neutrality,” “safety,” “regression,” “surface,” “negativism,” “suggestion.

Former Editor, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association; Professor of Psychiatry and Psychoanalysis, Emory University.

Arnold Richards: Collected Papers, Volume 2

Psychoanalysis: Perspectives on Thought Collectives: More Selected Papers by Arnold Richards, Volume 2. Edited by Arthur Lynch. New York: International Psychoanalytic Books, 2017, xl + 340 pp., $35.00 paperback.

Susan Kolod

Susan Kolod

330 West 58th Street, Suite 204
New York, NY 10019
Email: s.kolod@wawhite.org

Susan Kolod   Faculty, William Alanson White Institute; Co-Chair, Committee on Public Information, American Psychoanalytic Association.

In this second volume of his collected essays, Psychoanalysis: Perspectives on Thought Collectives, Arnold Richards identifies some of the most important forces that came together to create psychoanalysis, with particular emphasis on the development of psychoanalysis in the United States. He illustrates how these same forces are responsible for the deep schisms and institutionalized exclusionary practices that plague the field today. The essays provide illuminating historical data, important for contemporary psychoanalysts to consider. Cicero made the point that to be ignorant of what occurred before you were born is to remain always a child. Likewise, remaining ignorant of the forces that have shaped the development of psychoanalysis results in a superficial and simplistic understanding of controversies in the field.

This volume contains nineteen essays written between 1990 and 2016. Most are by Richards; a few are written in collaboration with other authors (Paul Mosher, ArthurLynch, Janet Bachant). Editor Arthur Lynch contributes a preface. The topics are wide-ranging: the roots of psychoanalysis in Judaism; Freud's and Brill's impact on psychoanalysis in the U.S.; Freud's free clinics; the history of the certification process in APsaA1; psychoanalysis and the far left; and thoughts about the future of psychoanalysis. Several book reviews and keynote addresses are also included.

1The Board on Professional Standards (BOPS), dismantled in 2017, mandated that training and supervising analysts be certified by its certification committee before being appointed. In fact, at one point all graduates had to be certified within a few years of graduation or they would lose their membership in APsaA. It is now a matter of “local choice” which members of an institute can be designated as Training and Supervising Analysts.203

The Maternal in James Joyce

Tyrants of the Heart: A Psychoanalytic Study of Mothers and Maternal Images in James Joyce. By Michael Zimmerman. New York: International Psychoanalytic Books, 2017, 234 pp., $35.00 paperback.

Austin Ratner

Austin Ratner

96 Joralemon Street
Brooklyn, NY 11201
Email: austinratner@gmail.com

Austin Ratner   Novelist; author, The Psychoanalyst's Aversion to Proof; member, Committee on Public Information, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Mirror, mirror on the wall, who's the Freudest of them all? Literary critics from Lionel Trilling to Michael Zimmerman have often asked this question of modernist fiction writers, and have answered it with surprising unanimity. While Virginia Woolf, William Faulkner, Marcel Proust, and Franz Kafka all depict mental life in a Freudian way, it's usually James Joyce who wins the prize for “most Freudian of all the great twentieth-century writers” (Zimmerman, p. 185). Joyce would have sent the prize back. Though he read some of Freud's work and discussed psychoanalysis with knowledgeable friends in Trieste, like Italo Svevo—and though his work emphasized many of the same aspects of the psyche that psychoanalysis does—he denied being influenced by Freud, or for that matter, by any other living writer besides perhaps Henrik Ibsen. So what makes Joyce the most Freudian of modern writers?

At the outset of his original and illuminating study of maternal imagery in the work of James Joyce, Michael Zimmerman provides a clue in the form of a quote from Finnegans Wake: “I can psoakoonaloose myself any time I want,” Joyce wrote. The statement perhaps reflects Joyce's refusal to share any glory with Freud, but also points to the central role of self-analysis in his work. To be sure, Joyce's free-associative and fearlessly self-observing art grants readers special access to the writer's unconscious and instinctual mental life. And it yields a map of the transferential connections between past and present in the minds of Joyce's characters—a map of what Mark Shechner (1974) has called “the devious byways of a lifelong family romance” (p. 5).

The first psychoanalytic review that I (SE) wrote was an appraisal of a collection of Loewald's: I believe the review was titled “Loewald: A Friendly Critic from Within.” In that volume, Loewald was strongly critical of the ego psychology of that era. While I perceived him as a friendly critic, I was to learn that analysts like Arlow and Brenner did not find him quite as friendly. Morris Eagle has written two volumes that embody the appellation of a friendly critic. Yet, as with Loewald, there are many who will not find him as affable as my description of him. Eagle is clearly a psychoanalytic thinker, a theorist, critic, and researcher who values psychoanalysis and wants analytic theory to be clearly and coherently stated. For those of us who see ourselves as well-versed in the psychoanalytic literature, many will be surprised at the number of studies in the literature that we have either not read or not considered seriously in terms of the importance of their findings. For this reason alone, Morris Eagle's volumes are well worth the price. He has provided the reader what no other psychoanalytic volume that we know of has accomplished. He has done a remarkable job in assembling the literature relevant to the concepts he is examining and eloquently discusses the evidence for these concepts. Somewhat tongue in check, we would propose that the difficulty many will have with Eagle is that he actually wants to view and evaluate the empirical evidence that supports a concept or theory.

The two volumes are titled Core Concepts in Classical Psychoanalysis and Core Concepts in Contemporary Psychoanalysis.

Creativity and the Erotic Dimensions of the Analytic Field. By Dianne Elise. New York: Routledge, 2019, xvi + 314 pp., $140.00 hardcover, $42.95 paperback.

Alison Brown

Alison Brown

330 West 58th Street, Suite 513
New York, NY 10019
Email: ab2422@cumc.columbia.edu

Alison Brown   Psychology Externship Director, Chair of the Division of Psychology, and faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

My teenage daughter, struggling to make meaning of her science textbook, asked me to read to her. We curled up on the couch and passed the book back and forth, reading alternate paragraphs aloud. This bodily engagement recalled memories of bedtime stories and putting on plays. Soon, links between light refracted through prisms, the radiating communication of celestial bodies across spacetime, and the expansion of the universe were alive with meaning and available for use in thinking about homework problems. But this shared pleasure quickly gave way to a feeling of overawe. What is the universe expanding into, we worried. What does “into” even mean in the context of an infinite universe with dynamic multidimensional formations, or of dark energy, the invisible roiling force that creates space? We anxiously turned away from this overwhelming encounter with the sublime. Back on the couch, my daughter completed her problem set and I returned to reading a certain psychoanalytic book.

What, you might ask, does this cosmic-domestic scene have to do with a review of Creativity and the Erotic Dimensions of the Analytic Field, Dianne Elise's brilliant and quietly revolutionary argument for analytic eroticism? I hope I can explain.

The transformative potential of erotic relationships and the contribution of eros to a life of knowledge and beauty have been recognized at least since Plato's Symposium. As happened between my daughter and me, and as sometimes happens in psychoanalytic work, eros can facilitate lively and creative thinking; but it can also lead to overwhelm and avoidance. Elise's book offers a passionate argument for how we might navigate eros's turbulent straits.

It is an ironic commonplace that psychoanalysis is inhibited about engaging the libidinal.

827

Peter Rudnytsky   Professor of English, University of Florida; Head, Department of Academic and Professional Affairs, and Chair, Committee on Psychoanalysis and the Academy and Committee on Confidentiality, American Psychoanalytic Association; Editorial Board member, JAPA, Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Psychoanalytic Psychology, Psychoanalytic Review, American Journal of Psychoanalysis, and Psychoanalysis and History.

What do we ideally look for in a psychoanalytic book? Speaking for myself, and I hope also for others, I would say we want a book that resonates deeply with what we already think we understand about what we do, but that extends our conceptual horizons in a way that continually surprises us with its originality while also striking us as inevitable, and indeed something we have always known, once we have had a chance to digest and metabolize what the author is saying.

Such a book, in my judgment, is Steven Stern's splendid addition to the Psychoanalysis in a New Key series. Initially, I must confess, I was not bowled over by the title. “Needed relationships”? It sounds almost banal. “Psychoanalytic healing”? Of course, that's our stock-in-trade. The subtitle can hardly be called poetic and seems at first glance to be plodding rather than inspired. On closer examination, however, I have come to appreciate that each word is carefully chosen and that the ensemble distills, in Erikson's phrase, Stern's “way of looking at things”—that is, what he, like all of us, ultimately has to offer his patients and the world.

Indeed, with the seemingly modest concept of “needed relationships,” Stern seeks to do nothing less than rise above the hubbub of competing theoretical discourses in contemporary psychoanalysis to construct an overarching framework for thinking about both the clinical process and the state of our field as a whole. The gist of Stern's position is what might be called an extreme nominalism, in the sense that while he agrees with Stephen Mitchell, for example, that all patients come to us seeking “a new relational experience uniquely fitted to their therapeutic needs and which contrast[s] in critical ways with the traumatizing experiences

Dwarakanath Rao   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Clinical Faculty, University of Michigan Department of Psychiatry; President, American Association for Psychoanalytic Education (AAPE).

The year 2020 ushered in a period of upheaval and reckoning. Into this time of pandemic anxiety, political extremism, and the blatancy of racism comes a book by Isabel Wilkerson destined to be an American classic. Her title, Caste: The Origins of Our Discontents, will doubtless remind psychoanalysts of Civilization and Its Discontents, in which Freud (1930) describes the development and resolution of the great battles between culture and the individual, and ending with the grim reminder of the ever present do-or-die tensions between self-love and the societal demand of love for fellow humans. Wilkerson's book has taken neighborhood reading groups, Oprah's book club, and its many reviewers by storm, all of whom are shaken by a strange, yet convincing account of a new theory of racism. Wilkerson weaves well-thought-out observations of three geographically and culturally disparate epochs of sanctioned subjugation, exploitation, and dehumanization—American racism, Nazi Germany, and the Indian caste system—and concludes that racism, anti-Semitism, and virtually every other form of discrimination is a psychological subset of casteism.

Wilkerson, a distinguished African American Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist and the best-selling author of The Warmth of Other Suns: The

The Unobtrusive Relational Analyst: Explorations in Psychoanalytic Companioning. By Robert Grossmark. New York: Routledge, 2018, xviii + 207 pp., $150.00 hardcover, $46.95 paperback.

Debra Neumann

Debra Neumann

4905 Earlston Drive
Bethesda, MD 20816
Email: drdebraneumann@protonmail.com

Debra Neumann   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, Contemporary Freudian Society.

Consider Bernard, who paces your office placing items from his pockets on various surfaces—a piece of crumpled paper here, his car keys there—at times baring his teeth and snarling at you. Or consider Ruben, who removes his shirt to show you a massive, roaring lion tattooed on his chest. Bernard and Ruben are two of the memorable patients Robert Grossmark describes to illustrate the thesis of his book, The Unobtrusive Relational Analyst. This book addresses a gap he finds in relational theory and technique posed by patients who seek psychoanalytic treatment but are unable to verbalize their innermost experiences. Their subjective sense of a separate self is either poorly developed or is hidden beneath the veneer of a competent, high-functioning “false self,” perhaps due to early trauma or emotional neglect. Grossmark elaborates on the nature of the regressed1 states of mind these patients present and the conditions needed for their relational analytic treatment.

Grossmark's thesis is that through a stance of unobtrusive psychoanalytic companioning, analysts enter the flow of the omnipresent enactments that pervade a treatment. Together, analyst and patient (in group work, analyst and members of the group) live through a variety of mutually experienced regressed states and dysregulations. Out of the seemingly primordial chaos of somatic sensations, actions, overwhelming anxiety, and confusion, capacities for self-other differentiation, affective regulation, and mentalizing emerge. There is no need for the analyst to interpret, clarify, or in any way impinge upon this flow of enactments, aside from establishing and maintaining a treatment frame and ensuring the patient's safety.

Some readers may be put off by this thesis, and by Grossmark's book. His ideas about the analytic stance and process required for successful treatment of inchoate mental states are engaging and at times provocative,

1Regression indicates the emergence of the patient's innermost being. “The patient is his regression” (p. 29).

Arthur Blank

Arthur Blank

8011 Glenmore Spring Road
Bethesda, MD 20817
Email: asbjrmd@aol.com

Arthur Blank   Faculty, Washington Baltimore Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, The George Washington University.

This is a dazzling book, drawing as it does on a great range in philosophy, history, and psychoanalysis, bringing together things often kept separate. It is of considerable value for the clinical analyst and for anyone with sociopolitical interests. A full accounting of its complexities is beyond a review. Rather, I found, the thing to do is to introduce the book, show what charms it holds for the reader, and urge us on to its more serious digestion in the reading.

The author was given a fine metaphor for this work, having suddenly lost hearing in one ear and undergoing a long process of learning to hear using a cochlear implant. She makes quick work of the distinction between listening and hearing: listening is the act of attention, hearing the resulting inflow.

The reader will be richly rewarded hearing Orange's explanations of the intersections of psychoanalysis, philosophy (especially ethics and phenomenology), and history, all organized around the concept of radical ethics. I understand her overarching definition of this to be valuing the other as much as or more than the self. This is probably the book's organizing principle, though it takes some thoughtful penetration to see how that works.

Challenges and Opportunities in Becoming One's Own Self: Commentary on Moss

Dorothy Holmes

Dorothy Holmes

10 Pinckney Colony Road
Suite 315, Bldg. 300
Bluffton, SC 29909
Email: crescent@gwu.edu

Dorothy Holmes  

Don Moss makes clear at the outset that his is not a “traditionally organized psychoanalytic text.” I was at once intrigued and made anxious by the unknown spaces and frames into which Don Moss casts us with his paper. If we do not have the anchors provided by our traditional psychoanalytic language and ways of thinking, with what do we anchor ourselves adequately to be able to experience and process the “affliction” Don invites us to study? I do not have an answer to that question for all of us. I can only say that, for me, intellectual curiosity about and a need to deeply understand Whiteness were enough for me to agree to discuss this paper, knowing that uneasy feelings would likely be stirred in me by plunging into such untrodden territory. Don recognizes that all the tools we use for inquiry have their limitations. If we rely only on our introspective capacity, we run into the limit that you can introspect only on what you already know. In exploring Whiteness, the search is for more than we already know and for more than we care to know. If we were to rely on theorized observation to study Whiteness, we would face the opportunities and biases of whichever theories we relied on. I would add that traditional psychoanalytic frames are particularly suspect because our history in psychoanalysis has been that we have appropriated those frames to perpetuate Whiteism. Here I use the word Whiteism to recognize Whiteness as action, in addition to its being a way or state of being. By use of Whiteism, we psychoanalysts have persistently and gratuitously privileged one theory over another and/or weaponized “theories” to hatefully exclude or degrade disfavored sexualities and races.

[[RunningHead]]

“I Do Not Have a Racist Bone in My Body”: Psychoanalytic Perspectives on What is Lost and Not Mourned in our Culture's Persistent Racism

Dorothy Holmes

Dorothy Holmes

10 Pinckney Colony Road
Suite 315, Bldg. 300
Bluffton, SC 29909
Email: crescent@gwu.edu

Dorothy Holmes  

whiteness, disavowal, racism

Racism is a disavowed marauding presence in the white psyche, a pathological formation that weakens psychic structures and creates a vulnerability to acting out that is hateful, discombobulated, and destructive. Examining it in its cultural, historical, and clinical contexts can enable its conscious ownership and mastery, which entails a process of mourning. Because racism is carried widely in the cultural surround and individually, it must be addressed by psychoanalysts. Cases are presented to show that racism is accessible to analysis in the consulting room.

[[RunningHead]]

Observations on Use of the N-Word in Psychoanalytic Conferences

Jyoti Rao

Jyoti Rao

870 Market Street, Suite 469
San Francisco, CA 94102
Email: jmrao@icloud.com

Jyoti Rao  

racial slur, racial trauma, deferred action, white supremacy, psychoanalytic culture, epistemic justice

In a frequently repeated group phenomenon, a racial slur is spoken in psychoanalytic conferences, after which a range of defensive responses emerge to counter acknowledgment of the meanings of having done so. After a discussion of the literature relevant to the use of slurs in psychoanalytic professional settings, Freud's concept of Nachträglichkeit, or deferred action, is used to identify and explore these events as a series of discriminatory gestures that evoke racial trauma. The defensive responses that emerge to protect the use of these gestures indicate ties to the traumatic legacy of slavery and to white supremacy as it appears in contemporary psychoanalytic culture. “Gestures of the open hand” are proposed, and their profound reparative potential is discussed. The intimate link between epistemic justice and psychoanalytic endeavors is delineated.

Psychoanalysis and Telepathic Processes

Thomas Rabeyron , Renaud Evrard and Claudie Massicotte

Claudie Massicotte

23 Boulevard Albert Premier
54000 Nancy
FRANCE
Email: thomas.rabeyron@gmail.com

Claudie Massicotte  

occultism, thought-transference, telepathy, psi, analytic field, interpsychic

Freud's writing on the topic of thought-transference stimulated controversy among analysts and original reflection on psychoanalytic understandings of the psyche. The notion of telepathy has also contributed significantly to the development of fundamental psychoanalytic concepts, including transference, projective identification, and primary forms of symbolization processes. The notion of telepathy, especially in light of current trends in post-Bionian and field theories, is used to outline an epistemological framework in which the clinical relevance of this notion becomes clear. Epistemological questions raised by telepathy and how this notion relates to the most originary and primary forms of the intersubjective relationship are addressed before questioning the conditions for the emergence of telepathy, its integration within contemporary psychoanalytic theory, and its ontological nature.

[[RunningHead]]

Revision of Drive Theory

Mark Solms

Mark Solms

Private Bag X3
Rondebosch, Western Cape 7701
SOUTH AFRICA
Email: mark.solms@uct.ac.za

Mark Solms  

affect, drive theory, Freudian theory, id, instincts, memory, neuropsychoanalysis, neuroscience, pleasure principle, repetition compulsion, free energy, homeostasis, self-organizing systems

Several deep revisions of Freud's theory of the drives are proposed: (1) Drives are conscious and are in fact the source of all consciousness. (2) Drive energy is equated with variational free energy and is therefore quantifiable in principle. (3) There are not two drives but many, seven of which may be described as “emotional” as opposed to “bodily” drives. (4) All drives are self-preservative or preservative of the species; there is no death drive at work in the mind. This means, at the mechanistic level, that all drives are homeostatic and anti-entropic. (5) The great task of mental development is to supplement instinctual predictions about how our multiple drive demands may be met and reconciled with each other. This work is done by learning from experience, mainly through voluntary behavior, which is governed by conscious feelings.

[[RunningHead]]

Intersubjectivity and Analytic Field Theory

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1
Pavia 27100
ITALY
Email: gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese  

intersubjectivity, Husserl, Merleau-Ponty, analytic field, Bion, enactment

Intersubjectivity is the central concept of the relational paradigm, the most widely employed in contemporary psychoanalysis. Yet we do not have a clear definition of it. Usually it is synonymous with “the interpersonal” and thus indicates the interaction that takes place between two already constituted subjects. In this sense it has little to do with the radical social theory of subjectivation suggested by the term, at least originally, in Husserl's philosophy. In the original meaning of intersubjectivity, as handed down by Husserl and later developed by Merleau-Ponty, the binary opposition between subjectivity and intersubjectivity is dissolved and transformed into a dialectic relationship. To formulate a clear and distinct, but above all specific, definition of intersubjectivity, we need to reclaim this intuition and translate it into coherent principles of technique. It is also essential to verify whether the models of psychoanalysis proffered as intersubjective actually satisfy this parameter. On the basis of these two simple principles, the variants of psychoanalysis that are labeled intersubjective can be placed along a continuum. Examples are given of “weak” and “strong” intersubjectivity. Paradigmatic of the latter pole is the post-Bionian theory of the analytic field.

responseSet.title: The Paternal Function in Anorexia Nervosa
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Paternal Function in Anorexia Nervosa

Tom Wooldridge

Tom Wooldridge

536 Mission Street
San Francisco, CA 94105
Email: twooldridge@ggu.edu

Tom Wooldridge  

anorexia, paternal function, fathers, agency

The role of the father, both flesh-and-blood and symbolic, is explored in a subset of families of patients with anorexia nervosa. In these families the mother's narcissistic investment in her child makes separation-individuation difficult. A factor potentially influencing whether the child goes on to develop anorexia nervosa is the strength of the paternal function, which optimally helps the child learn how to appropriately deploy his aggression in the service of separation-individuation and as a means of developing “the experience of agency”: the phenomenological experience of oneself as having an intentional impact. The role of the paternal function in developing the experience of agency is illuminated by the metaphor of rough-and-tumble play, which encapsulates the kind of experience with early objects that facilitates or forecloses the child's capacity for experiencing agency. In the families of these patients, the father is frequently described as passive or absent and the paternal function as compromised, which arguably leads the anorexic-to-be to relegate his experience of agency to his body, which he subjugates through omnipotent control.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Symmetry and Asymmetry in the Analytic Process: Reply to Commentaries

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1
Pavia 27100
ITALY
Email: gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese  

I would like to begin by thanking the anonymous readers of my article and the editors of JAPA for helping me improve it in the evaluation process, and the latter for asking me if I would allow colleagues to comment on it and for me to then respond. I also thank Donna Orange and Lewis Kirshner for their comments. I am honored that such well-known and respected authors have accepted the invitation of the JAPA editors. Since my essay is already long and demanding, I intend to respond to them as briefly as possible and only on the most relevant and controversial issues.

A careful reading of my paper would show that I do not use “weak” and “strong” as judgments of merit, but only descriptively, just as I might say “less or more inclusive” with respect to a factor or element X. There are situations in which a weak light is much better than a strong light and vice versa, for instance at the cinema or theater or restaurant, as well as, in analysis, if we think metaphorically of Enlightenment reason. It is no coincidence that one of the sources of inspiration for field theory was an Italian philosophical current called “weak thought” (Vattimo 1981; Vattimo and Rovatti 2010), where the adjective weak is used positively to indicate a modern or postmodern sensibility. The central thesis is the crisis of a “strong” notion of the subject as the repository of knowledge in which there is correspondence between words and things, thus close to an absolute idea of truth. Hence, for example, the “post-metaphysical” idea of “unsaturated interpretation.”

By the way, the judgment on the strengths and weaknesses of the various models could not be derived from a single element of a metapsychological order.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Personal Odyssey Through Psychoanalytic Process and Presence

Fred Pine

Fred Pine

30 West 60th Street
New York, NY 10023-7910
Email: pinef99@gmail.com

Fred Pine  

The work of psychotherapists and psychoanalysts is deeply influenced by their character and personality, as well as their overt life experiences. Since I can best illustrate this with the case I know best, myself, this will be a very personal paper. I give myself permission to write it because it is a subject not often written about in spite of its obvious importance. In the very month of my retirement after sixty-six years of practice and after receiving a diagnosis of terminal cancer, I find myself thinking about the evolution of the way I work. I am struck by the mix of what was originally learned, what was experientially gathered through clinical practice, what emerged through other professional but not directly clinical experiences, and, additionally, deeply personal factors. I feel it is of some general value to lay this out because, in one form or another, it is undoubtedly true that all of us are shaped by numerous factors beyond our psychoanalytic training. Many years ago, when I was writing up extensive clinical material from a patient, material that included highly personal sexual fantasies, and asked him for permission to publish it (with disguise and after he had read it), he said: “Sure! Doesn't everybody have that junk?” All of us, I believe, have this “junk” also, but it is certainly not only “junk”; it can productively shape our style of work, our commitment to the work, and our individual creativity in the work. Of course, it can also create problems and interfere.

Professor Emeritus, Department of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine; Adjunct Professor, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: On Having Whiteness
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Having Whiteness

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place, 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
Email: dbmossmd@gmail.com

Donald Moss  

racism, envy, groups, aggression

Whiteness is a condition one first acquires and then one has—a malignant, parasitic-like condition to which “white” people have a particular susceptibility. The condition is foundational, generating characteristic ways of being in one's body, in one's mind, and in one's world. Parasitic Whiteness renders its hosts' appetites voracious, insatiable, and perverse. These deformed appetites particularly target nonwhite peoples. Once established, these appetites are nearly impossible to eliminate. Effective treatment consists of a combination of psychic and social-historical interventions. Such interventions can reasonably aim only to reshape Whiteness's infiltrated appetites—to reduce their intensity, redistribute their aims, and occasionally turn those aims toward the work of reparation. When remembered and represented, the ravages wreaked by the chronic condition can function either as warning (“never again”) or as temptation (“great again”). Memorialization alone, therefore, is no guarantee against regression. There is not yet a permanent cure.

responseSet.title: Black Rage: The Psychic Adaptation to the Trauma of Oppression
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Black Rage: The Psychic Adaptation to the Trauma of Oppression

Beverly Stoute

Beverly Stoute

2801 Buford Highway NE, Suite 490
Atlanta, GA 30329
Email: beverlystoutemd@mail.com

Beverly Stoute  

Black Rage, race, racism, oppression, trauma, transgenerational trauma, mass trauma, slavery, racial trauma, Freud, Hannibal

Integrating the story of a young Freud's racial trauma with a novel application of the concept of moral injury has led to a realization and conceptual formulation during the pandemic uprisings of the mental construct of Black Rage as an adaptation to oppression trauma. As formulated here, Black Rage exists in a specific dynamic equilibrium as a compromise formation that is a functional adaptation for oppressed people of color who suffer racial trauma and racial degradation, an adaptation that can be mobilized for the purpose of defense or psychic growth. Black Rage operates as a mental construct in a way analogous to the topographical model, in which mental agencies carry psychic functions. The concept of Black Rage is crucial to constructing a theoretical framework for a psychology of oppression and transgenerational transmission of trauma. Additionally, in the psychoanalytic theory on oppression suggested here, a developmental line is formulated for the adaptive function of Black Rage in promoting resilience in the face of oppression trauma for marginalized people.

[[RunningHead]]

Peter Pan, Wendy, and the Lost Boys: A Dead Mother Complex

Robert White

Robert White

64 Trumbull Street
New Haven, CT 06510
Email: robertswhite@comcast.net

Robert White  

death instinct aggression, depression, narcissism, André Green, Peter Pan, James Barrie, dead mother

Melanie Klein and André Green offer competing descriptions of primitive mental development. The former emphasizes the need to control internal objects through splitting and projective identification, while the latter emphasizes a narcissistic retreat from objects through progressive deadening of the self. To bridge these theoretical differences a spectrum of fantasies is proposed ranging from reanimation (bringing deadness back to life) to reparation (healing damage caused by paranoid attack). Clinically, alternations between these two defensive patterns occur, acting together to avoid painful anxieties. The interplay of these defenses is illustrated by a dream drawn from clinical practice, from the life of James Barrie, and from his fictional creation Peter Pan.

[[RunningHead]]

Then and Now: Revisiting Enid Balint's Before I was I: Psychoanalysis and the Imagination

Before I Was I: Psychoanalysis and the Imagination. By Enid Balint, edited by Juliet Mitchell and Michael Parsons. New York: Guilford Press, 1993, 248 pp.

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

36 Highsett
Cambridge CB2 1NY
UNITED KINGDOM
Email: parsonstmc@gmail.com

Michael Parsons   Training Analyst, British Psychoanalytical Society; member, French Psychoanalytic Association.

Enid Balint (1903-1994) was a prominent figure in the Independent Group of the British Psychoanalytical Society. She and her husband Michael also developed a group method (known internationally as “Balint groups”) which helps physicians understand unconscious aspects of their relationships with patients. In addition, Balint was a pioneer in marital therapy. She founded the Institute of Marital Studies, which later became Tavistock Relationships. In 1996, the Institute of Marital Studies established an annual lecture in her memory, and in January 2021 it was my honor to present the twenty-sixth Enid Balint Lecture.

This was a special pleasure because Enid was an important figure for me personally in my psychoanalytic life. She supervised my first training case; and when she and Juliet Mitchell were putting together a collection of her papers, she invited me to join the project as coeditor with Juliet. That collection became her book Before I Was I: Psychoanalysis and the Imagination. The invitation to give the Enid Balint Lecture offered a wonderful opportunity for a close rereading of the book, to see how it would strike me today, more than twenty-five years later.

I began reading at both ends of the book: with the introduction, which I wrote myself, and with Juliet's interview with Enid that closes it. The book was published in 1993, so I probably wrote the introduction in 1992, ten years after I had qualified as an analyst. I was interested to see what had struck me particularly in Enid's writing at that stage of my experience. One thing I evidently picked up on is the idea of “aliveness.

responseSet.title: Analytic Writing as a form of Fiction
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analytic Writing as a form of Fiction

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
Email: thomas.ogdenmd@gmail.com

Thomas Ogden  

Writing, like dreaming, is a medium in which I think and talk to myself in ways I cannot do in any other form. Also, like dreaming, it keeps me alive in my work as a psychoanalyst, for I find that I have to be creating something of my own as I am immersed in trying to help someone else create something of his or her own.

Writing an analytic essay is a very difficult thing to do and requires a great deal of time and effort and anguish. Part of the difficulty is that writing of any sort is autobiographical—after all, where do our feelings, thoughts, and responses originate other than in ourselves? Consequently, analytic writers must put their private world on the page. The better the analytic paper, the more this is the case. I am referring not to information about one's life, but to the life of the writing. An analytic writer, in the very act of writing, is not simply creating a work of art in a particular literary genre; he or she is engaged in a process of being and becoming more fully him- or herself.

Opening oneself up in the act of writing is not enough. One must do something original with one's writing. We must find a way of looking at a situation in our own way, a way that bears our own mark. This is where engagement with language comes in: one creates one's “own mark” solely through the use of language. But the problem facing writers of all sorts is that experience does not present itself in words. Nor do ideas present themselves in organized sentences and paragraphs, much less in the form of an article with an internal structure. One must create something new in order to convey experiences and develop ideas in words and sentences and paragraphs.

Supervising and Personal Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

221

responseSet.title: Sex and Prediction Error, Part 2: Jouissance and the Free Energy Principle in Neuropsychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex and Prediction Error, Part 2: Jouissance and the Free Energy Principle in Neuropsychoanalysis

John Dall'Aglio

John Dall'Aglio

211 Rockwell Hall
Pittsburgh, PA 15282
Email: dallagliojohn@gmail.com

John Dall'Aglio  

neuroscience, consciousness, repression, real, jouissance, Lacan

Jouissance refers to an excess enjoyment beyond (yet tied to) speech and representation. From the perspective of some Lacanian analysts, jouissance is precisely what testifies against any relationship to the brain—jouissance “slips” out of cognition. On the contrary, it is argued here that jouissance has a central place in contemporary neuropsychoanalysis. In part 1 of this series the metapsychology of jouissance was presented in relation to the real and symbolic registers. Here, in part 2, Mark Solms's neuropsychoanalytic model of Karl Friston's free energy principle is summarized. In this model, “predictions” aim to resolve prediction errors—most notably, those signaled by affective consciousness. “Surplus prediction error”—prediction error that arises at the point where the predictive model fails—is proposed to be a neural correlate of jouissance. This limit within prediction is analogous to the real as a structural negativity within the symbolic.

responseSet.title: Sex and Prediction Error, Part 3: Provoking Prediction Error
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex and Prediction Error, Part 3: Provoking Prediction Error

John Dall'Aglio

John Dall'Aglio

211 Rockwell Hall
Pittsburgh, PA 15282
Email: dallagliojohn@gmail.com

John Dall'Aglio  

neuroscience, surprise, jokes, jouissance, technique, Lacan

In parts 1 and 2 of this Lacanian neuropsychoanalytic series, surplus prediction error was presented as a neural correlate of the Lacanian concept of jouissance. Affective consciousness (a key source of prediction error in the brain) impels the work of cognition, the predictive work of explaining what is foreign and surprising. Yet this arousal is the necessary bedrock of all consciousness. Although the brain's predictive model strives for homeostatic explanation of prediction error, jouissance “drives a hole” in the work of homeostasis. Some residual prediction error always remains. Lacanian clinical technique attends to this surplus and the failed predictions to which this jouissance “sticks.” Rather than striving to eliminate prediction error, clinical practice seeks its metabolization. Analysis targets one's mode of jouissance to create a space for the subject to enjoy in some other way. This entails working with prediction error, not removing or tolerating it. Analysis aims to shake the very core of the subject by provoking prediction error—this drives clinical change. Brief clinical examples illustrate this view.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Dall'Aglio

Mark Solms

Mark Solms

Private Bag X3
Rondebosch, Western Cape 7701
SOUTH AFRICA
Email: mark.solms@uct.ac.za

Mark Solms  

I am grateful to John Dall'Aglio for this enlightening set of papers and to the editors of JAPA for affording me the opportunity to respond to them. I very much agree with Dall'Aglio's view that “the brain is a potential common ground for different psychoanalytic schools” (p. 694)—indeed, that was one of the raisons d'être of Neuropsychoanalysis. It is perhaps not surprising, then, that his translation of Lacan's concept of jouissance into the language of neuroscience enables me to understand it in a way I never did before. That can only be a good thing; I would not otherwise have appreciated how deeply Lacan understood a driving force in mental life that has only recently come to occupy center stage in affective and computational neuroscience.

I particularly want to commend Dall'Aglio's courage in undertaking this translation, because proponents of our different psychoanalytic schools do not always welcome such exercises; generally, they prefer to foster the splits that flow from the “narcissism of minor differences.” I hope his good deed is not punished too harshly.

The argument presented in these papers pivots on a puzzle that greatly exercised Freud (1905), from the moment he equated pleasure with decreasing drive tension: namely, the fact that sexual excitement (i.e., increasing drive tension) is experienced as pleasurable:

Infantile sexuality does not aim at the reduction of [drive] tension to derive pleasure. Rather, it seeks a strange enjoyment in the increase of tension, in its excess. The quintessential example is the stimulation of the erotogenic zones, such as the oral and anal orifices. Freud [1905] discerns an enjoyment in the increased stimulation of these zones, without seeking discharge of the tension [Dall'Aglio, p. 696].

Director of Neuropsychology, University of Cape Town and Groote Schuur Hospital; Co-Chair, International Neuropsychoanalytic Society; Chair of Research, International Psychoanalytical Association; Science Director, American Psychoanalytic Association.

responseSet.title: Authority and Freedom
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Authority and Freedom

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

36 Highsett
Cambridge CB2 1NY
UNITED KINGDOM
Email: parsonstmc@gmail.com

Michael Parsons   Training Analyst, British Psychoanalytical Society; member, French Psychoanalytic Association.
conscience, sociopolitical issues, ethical issues, feminism, true and false self, subjectivation, ego, superego, identity, object relations, internal/external

Authority and freedom are connected, and both have external and internal forms. External authority, political or institutional, can impinge on internal freedom in ways that matter to psychoanalysts. Internal freedom requires an internal authority that can be trusted. The superego is an external authority masquerading as an internal one. The ego, with its compliance toward the id, the superego, and external reality, operates like a false self in the psyche. In Lacanian terms, it is of necessity alienated from itself. By contrast, the true self as described by Winnicott, and the “subject” as theorized especially in French psychoanalysis, represent an authentic conscience that allows us to become the authors of our at one's authority. This implies two sorts of freedom: freedom from narcissistic self-investment, and the forward-looking freedom of psychic growth. The latter occurs only in a context of relatedness to others, and it entails caring for the interests of others—if necessary at one's own expense. This vision of human beings as having an innate impetus toward psychic growth makes psychoanalysis a fundamentally optimistic endeavor. Clinical and nonclinical examples show, however, that it demands a perpetual readiness to let go of apparent certainties.

responseSet.title: Murderous Racism as Normal Psychosis: The Case of Dylann Roof
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Murderous Racism as Normal Psychosis: The Case of Dylann Roof

Alan Bass

Alan Bass

27 West 96th Street, 1F
New York, NY 10025
Email: bassaj@aol.com

Alan Bass  

violence, narcissism

Dylann Roof killed nine people in a Black church in 2015 in order to start a “war” between the white and Black races. This case is used here to develop a psychoanalytic theory of murderous racism, and even genocide. Major concepts from Freud (self-preservation, hatred, narcissism, life and death drives, delusion), Klein (projective identification), and Bion (psychotic and nonpsychotic parts of the personality) are employed. Particular attention is given to the hearing to determine Roof's capacity to represent himself after he dismissed his lawyers, who wanted to use an insanity defense to avoid the death penalty. This hearing was confronted with an undecidable dilemma, which led to legal cooperation with Roof's suicidal impulses. The deconstructive approach to undecidability, and its use in questions of law, especially as concerns the relation between psychoanalysis and the death penalty, leads to tentative recommendations for the prevention of racist murder.

responseSet.title: Commentary on Civitarese
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Civitarese

Donna Orange

Donna Orange

570 Mayflower Road
Claremont, CA 91711
Email: donna.orange@gmail.com

Donna Orange  

Answering a question with a question (Aron and Henik 2009), as my Jewish colleagues teach me to do, can challenge the most devoted hermeneut or phenomenologist. When asked to comment on Giuseppe Civitarese's paper, I faced the question whether and how to respond to this scholarly, dense, and intriguing text. Temptations abounded: from listing my many points of agreement to commenting on the sneaky charm of Civitarese's allusion to Pirandello's Six Characters in Search of an Author, a favorite of mine too. Altogether the honor of being so invited and so challenged intrigued me; responding requires me to acknowledge the incompleteness of what it will be possible to take up, and of the questions that time, space, and my own limitations will allow me to frame. My questions, I hope, will not mask my admiration. Thank you to this author and to the editors of JAPA for obliging me to question my previous beliefs, for rattling my cage.

Following three main topics—phenomenology, intersubjectivity, and post-Bionian field theory—Civitarese maps analytic intersubjectivity onto a spectrum of versions from weak (individual-oriented) through strong (field-oriented), characterized by a focus on “the transindividual essence of subjectivity” (p. 854). Psychoanalytic intersubjectivity is strong, he claims, if “(1) it is based on a radical social theory of how the psyche is born; and (2) it develops a clinical technique consistent with these assumptions” (p. 854). He regards his preferred variant (“post-Bionian”) as the best, perhaps the only legitimately strong, intersubjectivity (though he suggests that Thomas Ogden's probably also qualifies). All others are more or less weak. We can see that his scale resembles that of Greenberg and Mitchell (1983) in their valuation of psychoanalytic theories as more or less relational, setting the stage for all of Mitchell's later work (1988, 1993, 1997, 2000). My discussion will follow the path of Civitarese, raising questions along the way, but also adding the dimension of the ethical, of which he too has written in another context (Civitarese 2011).

Mitchell Wilson  

JAPA benefits greatly from the careful and dedicated work of its Associate Editors, who write many of the decision letters to authors and shepherd papers through the review process. This past year Anne Erreich, Jane Kite, Katie Lewis, Lynne Zeavin, Stephen Portuges, and Donnel Stern continued their outstanding work as Associate Editors. Nathan Kravis stepped down after nearly a decade of exemplary work in that role. Greg Rizzolo and Sarah Ackerman joined the Associate Editor team in 2020. Peter Rudnytsky has been Acting Associate Editor on papers as well. Anne Adelman and Jennifer Stuart are co-editors of the JAPA Review of Books, and Rachel Boué Widawsky is Associate Book Review Editor for Foreign Books. We thank all of them for their efforts on behalf of JAPA.

We say goodbye to the following Editorial Board members who made significant contributions to the journal during the past three years and who rotated off the Board on December 31, 2020: Daria Columbo, Henry J. Friedman, Axel Hoffer, Kay Long, Kerry Kelly Novick, Eslee Samberg, Michael E. Shulman, and Alan Sugarman. The following colleagues have been appointed to the JAPA Editorial Board for the term January 1, 2021, through December 31, 2023: Philip Blumberg, William Gottdiener, Francisco González, Leslie Keith (former Editorial Associate), Alfred Margulies, Seth Pittman, Peter Rudnytsky, Michelle Stephens, Beverly Stoute, and Lyn Yonack. [[RunningHead]]

The Writing Cure

Sandra Buechler

Sandra Buechler

340 West 86th Street, #3D
New York, NY 10024
Email: sbuechler2@msn.com

Sandra Buechler  

Most people don't understand the loneliness of the analyst. After all, we are never alone. Every forty-five (or fifty) minutes the bell rings and, like good Pavlovian subjects, we respond. Whatever just transpired, be it inspiring, unexceptional, or tragic (among many other possibilities), we ready our minds for the next occupant. We are rented by the hour, a circumstance that links us to the oldest profession. Constantly partnered, we are yet in danger of a form of loneliness that, at least in my experience, can be acutely painful. Writing offers me one of the very few effective balms.

What differentiates loneliness from aloneness, and makes it so hard to bear for all human beings? Fromm-Reichmann (1959) emphasizes that the profoundly lonely have given up hope that they will connect with others in the future. In addition, they see no redemptive purpose or meaning in their painful state. Loneliness can feel like an endless solitary confinement. Often it is accompanied by a shameful sense of meriting our isolation. Most of us, myself included, can call up bleak moments from early adolescence when loneliness felt more like a fate than a state.

Elsewhere (Buechler 1998, p. 138), applying Winnicott's thinking (1958) to the analyst's experience, I have suggested that the profoundly lonely analyst has failed to internalize a good enough “chorus” of empathic supervisors, colleagues, and other modulating influences. Profound loneliness can be the product of an overly harsh superego, criticizing every clinical interchange, alienating the analyst from him- or herself.

But even analysts who are well provided with empathic professional internal objects can suffer acute loneliness. With some patients, in some hours, we can feel they will never understand our experience as we do. We will be permanently alone with it. Perhaps we feel that the patient

[[RunningHead]]

Reconstructing the Depressive Position: Creativity and Style in Winnicott's “Concern” Paper

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

21 Vicksburg Street
San Francisco, CA 94114
Email: stephen.seligman@ucsf.edu

Stephen Seligman  

Winnicott, Menninger, stage of concern, Harold Bloom, depressive position, oedipus complex, fantasy, family, constructive activity, Melanie Klein, death instinct, aggression

D. W. Winnicott first presented his paper “The Development of the Capacity for Concern” (1963c) at the Topeka Psychoanalytic Society, which was closely tied to the Menninger Clinics, on October 12, 1962, during a longer visit to several institutes in the United States.1 The paper points toward the “revolution in our work” that he called for shortly before his death. Through a close reading of this “concern” paper, I elaborate its remarkable, if underestimated, place in this effort and show how his literary style reflects his distinctive metapsychological vision.

Winnicott's Challenging Style: A Literary Approach to Psychoanalytic Texts

Winnicott wrestled with the canonical analytic traditions that were most influential for him—Freud, Klein, and the ego psychologists.

1In my other paper in this issue (pp. 451-468; see also Seligman in press), I emphasize the “concern” paper's relation to Anglo-American ego psychology, viewed in the special circumstances of Winnicott's visit to the United States in 1962, and especially to Topeka. On this U.S. visit, Winnicott presented his paper on “communicating and not communicating” (1963a) in San Francisco, his “personal view of the Kleinian contribution” in Los Angeles (1962b), and his paper “Dependence in Infant-Care, in Child-Care and the Psycho-Analytic Setting” (1963b) in Boston.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, University of California, San Francisco; Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Clinical Professor, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Submitted for publication April 6, 2021.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mirror, Mirror: Freud and the Reversible Self-Object Dyad

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

Atlanta GA 30322
Email: rpaul@emory.edu

Robert Paul  

Freudian theory, drive theory, object relations, self-object dyad, mirror relation, relational theory

Freud is often negatively contrasted with object relations and relational theorists for holding to a metapsychology in which drives are understood as innate and predetermined in their development, are thought to follow the pleasure principle in a “hydraulic” manner, and are not seen as influenced by real objects. While that theory is certainly one dimension of Freud's thinking, it is paralleled by another, quite different model positing a “mirror” relation in which affectively charged reversible self-object dyads, as opposed to purely internal dynamics, are the constituent elements of mental life. This view, more compatible with theories placing greater emphasis on the constitutive role of self-object relations, may further the reconciliation of Freudian and object-relational theories.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Not Final, Not Complete”: Winnicott's Remarkable Presentation on Communication, San Francisco, October 8, 1962

Peter Goldberg

Peter Goldberg

909 Ordway Street
Albany, CA 94706
Email: petergoldberg@restio.org

Peter Goldberg  

communication, speech, omnipotence, Winnicott, communing

In 1962 D. W. Winnicott visited San Francisco, his one and only visit to the city. There he presented a paper on the topic of communication, which for years and decades after was mistakenly thought to be the prepublication text of the well-known paper “Communicating and Non-Communicating Leading to a Study of Certain Opposites,” published the following year (Winnicott 1963a), a gem of a paper that would become especially beloved for many of his readers. Only recently was a typewritten manuscript of the actual presentation found in the archives of the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.1 Clearly a forerunner of the published paper, the San Francisco presentation (Winnicott 1962a) is nonetheless a distinctive contribution in its own right.

A dearth of contemporary accounts of Winnicott's visit to San Francisco makes it hard to know how his ideas were received, but the newly discovered manuscript provides a fascinating picture of what he was trying to do in his presentation.

1At the top of the manuscript's opening page, Winnicott had handwritten the words “Not final, not complete,” which I have taken as my title for this paper.

Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Chair of Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

Submitted for publication March 25, 2021.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lee Jaffe's Unifying Approach to Psychoanalysis

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

990 Highland Drive, Suite 104A
Solana Beach, CA 92075
Email: sugarmanalan@gmail.com

Alan Sugarman  

Lee Jaffe's presidential plenary paper demonstrates his career-long emphasis on unifying psychoanalysis and its theories. His use of Freud's various technical concepts to make psychoanalytic theory applicable to talking psychotherapies of all kinds, analytic and nonanalytic, is a notable achievement and a useful pedagogical tool. By valuing common ground and unification, he aligns himself with luminaries such as Wallerstein and Rangell, who had similar goals. In keeping with these values, I offer a schematic outline of what the sort of integrated model valued by Jaffe might include.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fredric N. Busch Comments on Michael Diamond's “Return of the Repressed”

Fredric Busch

Fredric Busch

65 East 76th Street, #1B
New York, NY 10021
Email: fnb80@aol.com

Fredric Busch  

I want to commend Michael J. Diamond's effort in JAPA 68/5 (“Return of the Repressed: Revisiting Dissociation and the Psychoanalysis of the Traumatized Mind') to integrate (or encompass) a range of psychoanalytic theories and clinical interventions. As Diamond points out, the responses of the commentators, despite their insight and erudition, are concerning, given the vehemence of their critiques and the suggestion that there is a “right” model and clinical approach for most patients. The responses highlight long-standing schismatic tendencies of various psychoanalytic schools, to the detriment of a treatment that requires further development of its research and scientific base. For those new to the field, these papers could suggest we are in a muddle, with no clear way to determine which of several disparate approaches are most appropriate in a given clinical circumstance. I maintain that an integrated model is more theoretically and clinically sound and enables a more constructive engagement in dialogue among practitioners of different schools.

I have put forward models that attempt to integrate dissociated, unrepresented states and intrapsychic conflict, and have discussed relevant clinical approaches that overlap with those presented by Diamond (Busch 2017). I emphasize, as Diamond and others suggest, that individuals operate on multiple levels and self-states (“a dynamic amalgam of states of mind” [Diamond, p. 912]). Thus, their cognitions, affects, symptoms, and behaviors are simultaneously influenced by unrepresented and dissociative states, as well as by conflict and repression. Some of these states of mind are symbolized and readily accessible to consciousness, whereas others are more dissociated or sequestered. For example, patients with panic disorder can experience self-states in which they are highly aware of angry feelings and fantasies, and others, often related to traumatic developmental experiences, in which they lack any awareness of anger, a lack that contributes to panic symptoms. Access to painful states associated with trauma can be limited by dissociation, repression, and a lack of symbolic representation. Each of these mechanisms can contribute to how symptoms are expressed, and addressing these various factors can aid in identifying, symbolizing, and integrating traumatic states.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The “Oceanic Feeling” and Confrontation with Death

Richard Chessick

Richard Chessick

9400 Drake Avenue
Evanston, IL 60203
Email: r-chessick@northwestern.edu

Richard Chessick  

Freud, oceanic feeling, cipher

Freud's explanation of Rolland's “oceanic feeling” is reconsidered in the light of similar phenomena that occur in the face of impending death, such as the experiences described by Socrates in Plato's Phaedo, and the aesthetic and transformational experiences described by Christopher Bollas. These phenomena are included in what Karl Jaspers calls “ciphers.” Other examples are presented to indicate the need to consider such phenomena in human psychology, phenomena that have been neglected in psychoanalysis due to the profound but arbitrary influence of Freud's analysis of the “oceanic feeling,” an analysis based on the outmoded rigid assumptions of classical nineteenth-century science.

responseSet.title: Freud and Therapeutic Action Reconsidered: Current Applications
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Therapeutic Action Reconsidered: Current Applications

Lee Jaffe

Lee Jaffe

Lee Jaffe  

572

Freud aspired to develop psychoanalysis into a general science of the human mind. Even if contemporary psychoanalysts agree that in its heyday Freud's vision for psychoanalysis was overambitious, few today would argue that psychoanalysis is sufficiently known, understood, and utilized. In the eyes of the lay public, not to mention many professionals and academics trying to understand and treat mental illness, psychoanalysis is often unfamiliar, or, worse, it is misperceived as antiquated and unscientific. To counter these misperceptions, to reengage the public and the mental health community, it is important that we extend the diversity of psychoanalytic viewpoints into a more cogent body of psychoanalytic knowledge, and then find ways to demonstrate its current applications. What follows is such an effort; beginning with a classification of Freud's fundamental contributions to therapeutic action, followed by a demonstration of how this basic psychoanalytic knowledge is relevant to understanding all forms of psychotherapy, and to understanding different psychoanalysts' views of the analytic process.

Presidential plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, February 2021. Due to the author's illness, this paper was not in fact given as a plenary address. This publication is its first presentation. Dr. Jaffe passed away on June 20, 2021.

With the permission of Rowman and Littlefield, this paper is based on material from the author's How Talking Cures: Freud's Contributions to All Psychotherapies, chapters 2 and 6). Other chapters in the book present psychodynamic diagnoses and treatment recommendations; an illustration of therapeutic action over the course of a two-year intensive, transference-based exploratory psychotherapy; and therapeutic action in the treatment of couples. All rights reserved.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott's Idea of the False Self: Theory as Autobiography

Robert Ehrlich

Robert Ehrlich

1724 Buena Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94703
Email: reca1000@comcast.net

Robert Ehrlich  

false self, Winnicott, autobiography

One of the most important concepts developed by D. W. Winnicott was his idea of the false self. In the course of his work, he was often preoccupied with the need to maintain a sense of psychological vitality, which could be threatened in various ways. Threats occur throughout the life cycle as parents, siblings, friends, teachers, and other people impact the individual and contribute to the creation of the inner world of fantasy, which is also self-generated. These elements can work together or separately to undermine one's sense of vitality by the pressure to comply with a variety of demands, which can give rise to a false self. Winnicott's principal method of exploring this issue was through his clinical work, which was informed by personal struggles instrumental in shaping his thinking about the concept.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Body of One's Own: From Self-Cutting to the Cuts of Separation in an Adolescent Suffering from Traumatic Early Abandonments

Laura Balottin , Luca Quagelli and Maria Costantini

Maria Costantini

Via Venezia 8
35131 Padova
ITALY
Email: laura.balottin@unipd.it

Maria Costantini  

adolescence, self-cutting, bodily and psychic skin, trauma, transgenerational, representation

The treatment of adolescents suffering from early traumatic experiences inescapably involves the encounter with patients' concrete use of their bodies and actions. The clinical history of an adolescent girl reveals the relationship between traumatic transgenerational abandonments and self-cutting in the transference-countertransference relationship. Initially the patient's body and actions were the only way to communicate experiences that could not be conveyed in words and represented: the “skin for two” of the original psychosomatic envelope needed to be wounded, cut, broken concretely. The establishment of a boundary between internal and external, self and other, is the result of a complex process with roots in the quality of the encounter with the object. Gradually, in the encounter with the analyst, the young patient may construct a tenuous possibility of differentiation and begin to access the first outline of a representation of loss.

[[RunningHead]]

Female Leadership: Difficulties and Gifts

Harriet Wolfe

Harriet Wolfe

2105 Divisadero Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
Email: harriet.wolfe@ucsf.edu

Harriet Wolfe  

136

Talking with you on this occasion is a privilege based on my “retired” status as APsaA's immediate past president. That person is traditionally invited, six months later, to speak on a theme that is both personally meaningful and significant to psychoanalysis.1

When I conceived my topic for this plenary, “Female Leadership: Difficulties and Gifts,” I had in mind my experience as APsaA president and how my being a woman contributed to that experience. My thinking has evolved in the interim. My interest has become “The Feminine in Leadership,” and I will focus on that too in my remarks.

I hope to take you on my relatively recent journey without sacrificing my original goal: to inspire more women within APsaA to pursue leadership roles. The significant turn in my thinking has been in the direction of the feminine as a set of capacities that are not tied to biological sex and are critically important to both women and men who wish to become effective leaders in democratic, collaborative, forward-looking organizations.

There tends to be a conflation of female and feminine that does not serve us well. I will focus on the differences between female and feminine in relation to leadership using the lens suggested by Muriel Dimen (1997). She wrote about the fundamental interrelationship of psychoanalytic, feminist, and social theory. This triangular lens permits a disentanglement of terms and a move away from old categories that restrict us, including conflation of the female with the feminine.

1The world has changed since this plenary was presented, in part as a result of the Covid-19 pandemic. At a time when leadership has become an increasingly important and fraught topic, I hope what I have to say here proves helpful. The “ism” of interest in this plenary is sexism. Racism is not explicitly considered (as it would have been had the plenary been given more recently), but the theme of othering through patriarchy is certainly transferable to systemic racism.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 4

Philip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

In Volume 4, covering the years 1952-1955, we can see packed into a very brief and fast-moving time a Winnicott increasingly reaching into very deep clinical spaces and theorizing with confidence. No wonder, then, that Dominique Scarfone (as the editor of this volume) and Ofra Eshel (as the reviewer) propose that we read Winnicott slowly. As we track this unfolding career, we see exactly how Winnicott's deep, careful observation—as he moves from pediatrics to psychoanalysis—gives him an astonishingly rich base on which to work and think about psychotic process and the necessary, yet fraught, work of regression.

Eshel makes a clear case for the revolutionary aspects of Winnicott's work on regression, both in its links to earliest forms of subjectivity (and the potential for catastrophic early damage) and the clinical transformations in the analyst that will facilitate growth and developmental transformation in the analysand. Creatively bringing new material to illuminate this process, Eshel allows us to experience the potency of regression along with the dreadful and dangerous path the analyst must tread alone in another person's night.

What Eshel terms Winnicott's “radical evolution” during these furiously productive years applies both to his theoretical work and to his sense of his own accomplishments. In stressing the burden and necessity of engaging patients' regressions, Winnicott repeatedly suggests the personal transformations that occur in the analyst that are inspired by those engagements. Perhaps in unfreezing the “failure situation” in his patients, Winnicott himself finally entered a space where he could sustain his deep love for Freud while simultaneously acknowledging his own difference and originality. The urgency, authenticity, and confidence that radiate from the papers in this volume indeed reflect this transformation.

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott's Clinical Theory of Regression: A Radical New Opportunity Through the Fundamentals

Ofra Eshel

Ofra Eshel

12a Anatot Street
Tel Aviv 690801
ISRAEL
Email: eshel211@bezeqint.net

Ofra Eshel   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, Israel Psychoanalytic Society; Vice President, International Winnicott Association; Head, Independent Psychoanalysis: Radical Breakthroughs, advanced studies of the Program of Psychotherapy, Sackler Faculty of Medicine, Tel Aviv University.

Volume 4 of Winnicott's collected works (1952-1955) covers the period in his writing in which he developed, presented, struggled with, and even pleaded for a true understanding of his unique way of thinking about the psychoanalytic study of psychosis and early psychic development, and the closely related crucial issue of regression in analytic work. It also contains the first published version of “Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena,” from 1953. In his personal life, Winnicott's marriage to Clare Britton on December 28, 1951 (mistakenly noted in Volume 5 as 1955), with whom he had been working since 1943, marked the beginning of the lively last twenty years of his life.

It is beyond my scope here to address the broad and rich array of thoughts that arise on reading this volume and Dominique Scarfone's evocative introduction, which proposes a different way of approaching Winnicott—reading him slowly in order to capture the original mode, logic, and movement of his highly personal psychoanalytic language in expressing his ideas, and in this way putting aside one's previous knowledge of “Winnicott” rather than rushing toward what is already familiar. I have chosen to examine slowly the radical evolution of Winnicott's clinical thinking into the launching of what I consider a revolutionary approach in clinical psychoanalysis. This is an essential point for me (Eshel 2013b, 2017b, 2019). Winnicott has exerted a profound influence on the theory and practice of clinical psychoanalysis over the past sixty years, and his groundbreaking ideas have been widely investigated by psychoanalysts and psychotherapists around the world. Yet it seems to me that the revolutionary meaning of his most radical ideas has, in certain ways, been evaded, underestimated, or criticized and rejected.
