Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

4701 Willard Avenue, #204
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
Email: richard.waugaman@georgetown.edu

Richard Waugaman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Georgetown University; Training and Supervising Analyst Emeritus, Washington Baltimore Psychoanalytic Institute.

Riding in a cab from Penn Station to the Waldorf Astoria for the 2017 meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, my wife and I listened to our driver's stories about himself; he claimed to be a powerful figure in local New York politics—such an outspoken, progressive activist that local politicians were afraid of him. When we asked what he thought of then newly elected President Trump, our driver seemed to shrink into himself. He explained that he didn't feel comfortable criticizing Trump. Perhaps because of Trump's alleged Mafia ties, we wondered to ourselves? Yes, Mafia ties. Although Justin Frank does not mention this in Trump on the Couch (2018), it has long been alleged that Trump actively sought ties with the Mafia, more than other New York builders did. In 2013 Trump said to David Letterman, of mobsters he had met, “They happen to be very nice people” (Bruney 2020).

That conversation in the taxi alarmed me, and made me worry even more about the future of free speech in our democracy. A couple of days later—on January 21, the day after Trump's inauguration—my wife and I participated in the Manhattan Women's March. Here, by contrast, we saw reassuring evidence that women and others were not afraid to protest against Trump. A woman from Eastern Europe told us she had seen demagogues corrupt democracy in her country of origin, and she was disturbed by all the similar warning signs that Trump intended to do just that in the United States.

423

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott in America

Joseph Aguayo

Joseph Aguayo

11849 West Olympic Boulevard, Suite 202
Los Angeles, CA 90064
Email: joseph.aguayo@gmail.com

Joseph Aguayo  

With the publication of The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott (Caldwell and Taylor Robinson 2016), there has been considerable interest in drawing on the many newly available archival documents that help us fill in the picture of the psychoanalytic odyssey undertaken by Winnicott during his fruitful and generative career as a pediatrician/psychoanalyst. A range of scholarly contributions from a diverse, international group of analysts, each providing an “introduction” to Winnicott's corpus, have thrown fresh light on the intellectual and historical forces that shaped Winnicott's thinking and theorizing. To supplement this work, for the greater part written by Anglo-European analysts, I decided to gather a group of American analysts, also familiar with Winnicott's work, for a different kind of project: namely, tracing his impact in the wake of his numerous lecture visits to the United States in the 1960s.

With the aid of Nellie Thompson, chair of the History, Archives, and Library Committee at APsaA, a group of colleagues convened a panel, “Winnicott in America,” at the APsaA meetings in February 2019. The panel, on which I sat, included Peter Goldberg, Nirav Soni, and Karen Melikian as well. Our aim was to discuss Winnicott's visits to American psychoanalytic institutes in 1962, 1963, and 1968. Nellie Thompson chaired the panel.

Our panel was fortunate in this instance because both Lesley Caldwell and Amal Treacher-Kabesh, both members of the Winnicott Trust in

Editor's note: I asked Joseph Aguayo to write a few paragraphs that describe the provenance of the 2019 ApsaA Panel on Winnicott in America, and the book that will be published on that topic by Oxford University Press next year. The papers by Peter Goldberg and Stephen Seligman that follow Aguayo's remarks emerged as part of this larger effort. We at JAPA are grateful to have these writings by analysts who are not only well-versed in Winnicott's ideas and legacy but also have contributed in remarkably original ways to the leading edge of contemporary psychoanalytic thought.

[[RunningHead]]

A Tale of Two Winnicotts

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 5, 1955-1959. Edited by Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016.

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

64 Bilu Street
Tel Aviv 6425613
ISRAEL
E-mail: emanuel@psy.haifa.ac.il

Emanuel Berman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Israel Psychoanalytic Society; Professor Emeritus of Psychology, University of Haifa; faculty, Israel Winnicott Center.

Some years ago an image crossed my mind, and I wrote about “a tale of two Freuds.” What I meant was the tension between Freud the sensitive and open-minded listener to subjective individual experience, deeply influenced by literature and art, and Freud the ambitious conquistador who strove to make absolute generalizations about universal human nature (Berman 2003). Personally, I feel we should always be loyal to the first Freud, but examine critically the conclusions of the second.

My “tale of two Winnicotts” relates to another fundamental tension, between Winnicott the daring and original revolutionary and a Winnicott who often made enormous efforts to remain loyal to the heritage of classical psychoanalysis. This effort led at times to absurd statements; maybe the sharpest example is concluding his radically innovative paper about true and false self (Winnicott 1960; CW 6:1:22)1 with the sentence “As far as I can see it involves no important change in basic theory.”

When I explored volume 5 of the Collected Works, as I was asked by the editors, I had this tension in mind. The period covered here, 1955-1959, reviewed broadly and thoughtfully by Jennifer and Marcus Johns in their introduction to the volume, follows on two major changes in Winnicott's life: his gradual separation from the Kleinian group in the 1940s, and his marriage with his second wife Clare in 1951. Both of these changes increased to my mind his personal and professional autonomy. Within this period (in 1956) he was elected to his first three-year term as president of the British Psychoanalytical Society (the second term started in 1965).

1Out of respect to the great project of Winnicott's Collected Works, I identify all my Winnicott references with the marking proposed in this edition: 6 in this case refers to the volume (covering 1960-1963), 1 to the section within it (covering 1960), and 22 to the item within that section.621

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Dall'Aglio and Solms

Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher

1491 Hopkins Street
Berkeley, CA 94702
Email: charlep@aol.com

Charles Fisher  

It is a pleasure to introduce the dialogue published here. John Dall' Aglio's three-part “Sex and Prediction Error,” Mark Solms's commentary, and Dall'Aglio's reply are together a Rosetta stone for two languages within psychoanalysis: Lacanian psychoanalysis and neuropsychoanalysis. Each language has its own vocabulary and syntax, and yet each is closely connected to core sources held in common. Each reaches beyond language to describe multiple layers of reality within psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice. Each rewards close study in its own right, but gaining access requires work. The dialogue here between Dall'Aglio and Solms carries out a meaningful bit of that work, illuminating two approaches to psychoanalytic thinking, while shedding light on psychoanalysis itself from two convergent perspectives.

Neuropsychoanalysis

Dall'Aglio's comprehensive contribution speaks for Lacanian neuropsychoanalysis. In itself, it is a massive work of integration, in which Dall'Aglio devotes an entire part to neuropsychoanalysis, including an explication of Solms's neuropsychoanalytic model of Karl Friston's free energy principle (Friston et al. 2010) and a defense of neuropsychoanalysis against charges of reductionism. Solms's commentary approaches the dialogue from the perspective of neuropsychoanalysis.

An accessible summary article, “What Is Neuropsychoanalysis?” by Mark Solms and Oliver Turnbull (2011)

Deputy Director, American Psychoanalytic Association Science Department and Co-Chair, Study Group on Psychoanalysis and Neuroscience; Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

[[RunningHead]]

Eating your Cake and Having it

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 6, 1960-1963. Edited by Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016.

Peter Goldberg

Peter Goldberg

909 Ordway Street
Albany, CA 94706
Email: petergoldberg@restio.org

Peter Goldberg   Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Chair of Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; faculty, Wright Institute, Berkeley.

Winnicott once said that no sooner would he begin reading a paper than he would want to write one himself. It is certainly an effect that reading his work can have on others as well. Yet at the same time it can be hard to pull oneself away from a Winnicott text. This is especially the case reading through Volume 6 of the Collected Works, where one can meander through the full range and variety of his output, with pleasurable surprises around every corner. All of this makes the position of reviewer challenging: how to somehow give an account of the protean thinking; and how to restrain oneself from going off on some spontaneous speculations of one's own?

The well-known innovations of Winnicott's world of thought—“play” and “transitional objects” and “holding environment”—have already entered the canon, and his work is quoted everywhere and by practically everyone these days. Yet the effect of reading this volume, which covers the period 1960-1963, is of encountering germinal ideas whose potential and full impact are yet to be realized. Maybe the not-yet-saturated quality of his writing is a reason why his thinking has remained so vital and claims the interest of so many.

This was not only a remarkably creative period for Winnicott, but a time when he expanded beyond his primary concern with child development and the clinical environment, writing highly consequential papers that proposed bold new formulations on communication and on the sense of aliveness and realness in self-experience. Always eschewing abstract systemization,1

1However, he often resorted to lists and summary shorthand. “Providing for the Child in Health and Crisis” (pp. 343-350) is an example of an entire paper laid out mostly in a bulleted shorthand format.789

[[RunningHead]]

Fear of Breakdown: Politics and Psychoanalysis. By Noëlle McAfee. New York: Columbia University Press, 2019, xx + 292 pp., $90.00 hardcover, $30.00 paperback.

Suzanne Benser

Suzanne Benser

555 East City Avenue, Suite 950
Bala Cynwyd, PA 19004
Email: sbenser@gmail.com

Suzanne Benser   Faculty, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia; Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Temple University.

Noëlle McAfee's Fear of Breakdown: Politics and Psychoanalysis was released in 2019 as rising nationalism and polarization evolved in tandem with deep social pathologies evident in the escalating racism in police violence, anti-immigrant policies, and white supremacist terrorism.

818

McAfee's book was awarded the Courage to Dream Prize by the American Psychoanalytic Association, honoring “the book that best promotes the integration of the academic and clinical worlds of psychoanalysis.” The book is even more relevant now, as the disparity in Covid mortality and vaccination patterns highlights the nation's political and racial fault lines. Though the triumph of nationalism in the U.S. may have been averted, the paranoia underlying the denial of the presidential election results by so many indicates that political sectarianism, barring significant systemic change, will likely continue gaining strength. A model for transformative systemic change is what McAfee lays out in her book.

It seems that as psychoanalyst citizens we can no longer insulate ourselves from the larger culture or look away from our participation in these systems. Our dependence on others to prevent the virus from spreading, and to be reliable sources of information while relying on our government to equitably and efficiently deliver vaccines, spotlights the reality of how interdependent we are. Attesting this interdependence, psychoanalytic scholarship and activism increasingly look to neighboring fields to see what we can learn, and what we have discounted and enabled. In similar fashion, fields such as critical theory have looked to psychoanalysis to understand human irrationality at times of crisis, as when much of Europe turned to fascism after World War I and now again, as the world turns to populism and other movements, putting the planet and its citizens in rising danger. “Cultural theory,” McAfee writes “has been more bent on diagnosis than remedy. Critical theory has been attuned more towards finding transcendent criteria. … It is time we start learning from each other. … A critical social theory informed by psychoanalysis, including the negativity that Freud identified, can focus on ‘the dynamic reworking of affect’ … which could make ‘social transformation possible’” (pp. 43-44).

Lewis Kirshner

555 Prinseneiland

Lewis Kirshner  

I enjoyed reading Giuseppe Civitarese's eloquent and conceptually rich paper. His remarkably clear presentation of Husserl and of his own perspective on intersubjectivity makes a powerful argument about the central issues facing contemporary psychoanalysis: what does intersubjectivity mean in practice and how best to conceive of it? My discussion has three parts. First I take up the paper itself, its organization, methods, and aims. Next I comment on the version of intersubjectivity that Civitarese advances. Last, I offer my own experience of learning about the concept and how it has influenced my practice in an ongoing process of growth.

Intersubjectivity and Analytic Field Theory

In his paper, Civitarese presents in some detail the development of Husserl's thought. In his early work, Husserl struggled to make sense of the notion of empathy as a way of connecting the subject with others around him. Most commentators would agree that this project was not successful. Many later theorists, in both psychology and philosophy, confronted the same problem (of other minds) and, it might be said, remain stuck in a tautological and tendentious use of empathy as a core notion. Cognitive neuroscience still debates how to solve the apparent problem of recognizing that we are surrounded by others with minds like our own. This difficulty of escaping the Cartesian dilemma of the isolated subject has been extensively challenged by phenomenologists, notably by Heidegger and by Husserl in his later work, recognizing that the subject begins life immersed in an intersubjective world. Civitarese masterfully unravels and explains Husserl's new approach, largely found in unpublished notes and manuscripts, in which he proposed that subjectivity and intersubjectivity originate together in a dialectical, prereflexive, transcendental unity. Obviously, this genre of formulation remains both extremely abstract and almost impossible to imagine in terms of infantile or neonatal life.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fred Pine: An Exemplary Analyst

Sarah Ackerman

Sarah Ackerman

5 Hunter Lane
Hanover, NH 03755
Email: ackerman@post.harvard.edu

Sarah Ackerman  

Fred Pine is a clinical psychologist with over sixty years in the practice of psychoanalysis. In this memoir, Fred attempts to bring the many facets of a well-lived life together to inform the trajectory of his work—to identify the shaping influences and their impact. Fred's account of his deep and abiding engagement with analytic theory and practice is inspirational, and I would argue that Fred himself is an exemplary figure in the history of psychoanalysis.

It will take you only a few sentences to perceive the candid and open style of this memoir, which immediately invites the reader into a personal relationship with Fred Pine, the person. Consider this opening:

The work of psychotherapists and psychoanalysts is deeply influenced by their character and personality, as well as their overt life experiences. Since I can best illustrate this with the case I know best, myself, this will be a very personal paper. I give myself permission to write it because it is a subject not often written about in spite of its obvious importance. In the very month of my retirement after sixty-six years of practice and after receiving a diagnosis of terminal cancer, I find myself thinking about the evolution of the way I work [p. 945].

Fred is letting us know that he is going to use his astute analytic eye to interrogate his own attraction to psychoanalysis, sharing childhood determinants, as well as, surprisingly, the role of Tourette's syndrome in influencing his career direction. As you will see, he does not stop there. By paper's end, we have been invited to accompany him in the complex process of closing his practice after his terminal diagnosis. Here we see a courageous and deeply connected man who has been able to put the needs of his patients first, even as mortality is nipping at his heels.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Co-leader, Psychoanalysis Study Group, Leslie Center for the Humanities, Dartmouth College; Supervisor, Psychiatry Residency Program, Geisel School of Medicine, Dartmouth College.

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Series of Reviews (Continued)

In this issue of JAPA Review of Books, we offer the seventh installment in a series on The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott, curated by guest editors Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris. In a review essay on Volume 7 (1964-1966), titled “Winnicott at Work,” Glen Gabbard brings us a Winnicott who, despite feeling wounded and at times overlooked or misunderstood by his colleagues, nonetheless worked tirelessly to educate the public and—significantly—to provide space for listening. During this period, Winnicott enriched our understanding of some of his most enduring and impactful ideas, particularly of the importance of preserving silence, at times, for the gradual emergence of patients' capacity to “recognize who they are.” As Gabbard states, “We have learned from Winnicott to listen carefully to the patient's perspective and recognize that patients are attempting in their own way to let us know who they are.”

A View of Relationships across Time

How do psychoanalytic concepts percolate into popular culture? Often their influence is pervasive but subtle; sometimes they are rendered as preposterous shadows of themselves. Occasionally, a psychoanalyst who is also a gifted writer—or a talented author with serious psychoanalytic chops—takes hold of a psychoanalytic concept and presents it in a way that captures the public imagination, helping others see the world through a psychoanalytic lens. In her book essay, Laura Whitman—a psychoanalyst who treats children, adolescents, and adults—looks at three works rooted in an essentially psychoanalytic view of development across the human lifespan. In The Rough Patch

[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott at Work

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 7, 1964-1966. Edited by Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016.

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

4306 Yoakam Boulevard, Suite 535
Houston, TX 77006-5831
Email: glen.gabbard@gmail.com

Glen Gabbard   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Baylor College of Medicine.

Myriad themes are featured in Volume 7 of The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott. In her thoughtful introduction to the volume, Anna Ferruta notes that the period it covers overlapped with his second term as president of the British Psychoanalytical Society. During the Controversial Discussions of 1942-1944, Winnicott was determined to avoid being assimilated by any of the three designated groups. Ferruta points out that Winnicott preferred to listen to the debates first and subsequently write letters explaining his personal views. He was clear that he did not wish to create a new school of thought. Nevertheless, one of the most striking aspects of this volume is the polemical tone he adopts in much of the discourse.

On the other hand, he also saw himself as an educator of the public. Far from being a critic of mothers and/or families, Winnicott was a strong advocate for them. Also noteworthy in this volume is that many of the contributions Winnicott offered were in response to requests by nonpsychoanalytic organizations. Ferruta suggests that it was a challenge for Winnicott to explain his usual independent thinking, while at the same time making a major effort not to come across as intolerant or detached.

The Winnicott letters in Volume 7 paint a somewhat painful picture of D.W.W.—one that reveals a vulnerability to feeling slighted by colleagues. His correspondence makes it clear that he often felt bypassed, unread, left out, misunderstood, and discounted. While colleagues spoke of his jovial nature, I have been struck by the opposite in studying Winnicott photographs over the years. He often appears haunted and distressed. I realize I am seeing a man who felt wounded by colleagues in the British Society. In his response to a letter from John O. Wisdom (CW 7:1:11)

life and death / stand face-to-face, cooing at each other— / … the braided nature of life and death, they pour /and empty each into the other …

—David Grossman (2011, p. 178)

This volume of Irma Brenman Pick's collected papers provides the reader the close-up experience of a gifted clinician going about her daily work; we are invited to observe, feel, and learn. The tone of her writing, steeped in theory, is of a theory that has been lived in, used fully and with love. She leans heavily on the work of Melanie Klein and at the same time has a distinctive voice, an analyst who has found her own way.
